entries.title: Introduction
entries.snippet: THE Bushmen were members of a division of the human species that in all probability once occupied the whole, or nearly the whole, of the African continent.

entries.snippet: The Hartebeest and the Eland are things of the Mantis ; therefore they have magic power. The Mantis is used to go with the Hartebeest when he walks about.

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_001
entries.snippet: Notebook on language (words and sentences) of Langeberg Bushmen, met by DB on Mount Temple farm. Some personal details relating to contributors.

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_002
entries.snippet: Notebook on language (words and sentences) of Langeberg Bushmen. Some personal details relating to contributors (the Bushmen she met on Mount Temple farm) including names and some genealogical details. Some pages loose.

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_003
entries.snippet: Notebook, preliminary pages unumbered in German script. Words and sentences. Much personal history.

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_004
entries.snippet: Notebook. Includes some words and sentences. Mostly personal history (genealogies, and places of of contributors and their relations). Customs, especially burial practises. A segment of a diary of DB.

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_005
entries.snippet: Notebook. Mostly words and sentences. Some personal history (some genealogies). Contributors at same location [?].

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_006
entries.snippet: Diary. A5 size. DB's journey to Kakia, Bechuanaland. Lots of blank pages. Not paginated by DB but has been paginated by library [1]-[165].

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_007
entries.snippet: Notebook. Mostly words and sentences, some personal information. Bechuanaland.

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_008
entries.snippet: Notebook. Notes and words, Masarwa.

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_009
entries.snippet: Notebook. Words and sentences and geneaologies, personal history. Bechuanaland.

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_010
entries.snippet: Reporters' Note Book. Naron language notebook. Words and sentences, some personal history. Reference made to recordings made by DB. Some information regarding customs and daily life.

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_011
entries.snippet: Reporters' Note Book. Naron language notebook. Short narratives concerning daily life and rituals - especially menstruation, marriage, birth, healing, rain, treatment of bones and hunting; some words and sentences.

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_012
entries.snippet: Naron & !kun notebook, Vol. I. Words and sentences. Some descriptions of daily life including names and uses of artefacts and relations with others; personal histories.

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_013
entries.snippet: A manuscript book. !kun or Auen and Naron notebook, volume II. Words and sentences, lots of history of the region (leaders, borders, wars), odd bits of personal history, some local description.

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_014
entries.snippet: A manuscript book. Auen and Naron notebook, volume III. Words and sentences, mostly the latter.

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_015
entries.snippet: A manuscript book. Auen and Naron notebook, volume IV. Words and sentences, mostly the latter.

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_016
entries.snippet: Naron & Auen notebook, Volume IV. Much personal history and of the inhabitants of the area. Considerable detail as to practises and rituals, especially songs and dances and work hunting and looking for veldkos.

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_017
entries.snippet: Naron notebook (volume V) Detailed description of dances, ghosts and spirits, burial of the dead, sorcery and magic arrows, addressing the rain, and related practices.

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_018
entries.snippet: Naron notebook (volume VII). Descriptions of daily life, contemporary life and personal history.

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_019
entries.snippet: Auen notebook, volume VIII. Much description of daily life, current life and personl history, including relations with others and the inhabitants of the area/settlement.

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_020
entries.snippet: Naron notebook, Vol. IX? (with Auen contributions too). Fairly fragmented accounts of contemporary life, history and building of the settlement, and personal history; much daily life, including illness, names of plants…

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_021
entries.snippet: Notes made in Angola[?], 1925. Originally a visitor's book (pre-1918) and includes signatures of numerous well-known people (first 64 pages). A scrap of paper written by the library says !O !kun.

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_022

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_023
entries.snippet: Notes made in Angola 1925. Words and sentences and personal history and history of the inhabitants of the area, and descriptions of objects and actions from daily life, as well as fragments relating to markings, and…

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_024
entries.snippet: Notes on rock paintings in caves in the Orange Free State c.1928, including comments on condition of the paintings, location of the caves and comparision between Stow's copies and originals.

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_025
entries.snippet: Notes on rock paintings in caves in the Orange Free State c.1928, including comments on the condition of the paintings, the location of the caves and comparisions between Stow's copies and originals, as well as notes as…

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_026
entries.snippet: Notes on rock paintings copied by Stow, and revisited by DB, mainly in the Eastern Cape near Queenstown. Notes about other paintings discovered as well as the farms they are found on and who to ask for directions.

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_027
entries.snippet: Record of D Bleek's visit to the Hadza of Tanganyika, 1930. Words and sentences with very fragmented narratives towards the latter half of the book.

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_028
entries.snippet: Record of D Bleek's visit to the Hadza of Tanganyika, 1930. Words and sentences and fragmented narratives towards the latter half of the book.

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_029

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_030
entries.snippet: Notes on |auni Vol.2, made at Frankenwald Research Station, Witwatersrand University (presumably, it doesn't say so in the notebook). Words and sentences and personal history, including some rough genealogies.

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_031
entries.snippet: A dictionary, A-Z. Language unknown, but likely to be Mbunela, as there is a reference to the Mbunela language in the notebook.

entries.title: Dorothea Bleek - Book BC151_A3_032
entries.snippet: Bushman genealogies, undated. Some brief descriptions and personal histories, such as where individuals worked, came from, who they married, how many children they had and so on.

entries.title: Jemima Bleek's 'Hottentot' interviews, 1879
entries.snippet: Thirty pages of interviews collected by Jemima Bleek in 1879 with a three 'Hottentot' informants (I, II and III), members of a family ('Lynx') sent to the house in Mowbray in error.

entries.title: Jemima Bleek's interviews with !nanni and Tamme, September to November 1879
entries.snippet: Thirty pages of interviews collected by Jemima Bleek in late 1879 with !nanni (XI) and Tamme (XII). The notebook contains mostly vocabulary, largely relating to animals.

entries.title: !ga ka Kkumm. The Frog's story (Or, The Frog, the Blue Crane, the Beetle, and !kuommain ǀka ǁkau) (Part I)
entries.snippet: The Frog, the Blue Crane, the Frog's husband, and the Beetle. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-32. 8812-8820).

entries.title: !gaunu
entries.snippet: !gaunu is also !guonni and is at the bee's hole. He bewitches (or 'stabs') people (to death) if they go to the hole where he is.

entries.title: !gaunu-tsaxau (the son of the Mantis), the Baboons, and the Mantis
entries.snippet: The son of the Mantis is killed by the Baboons, and restored to life by his father. This piece contains specimens of the manner in which the Bushman language is supposed to be spoken by baboons.

entries.title: !guerriten-dde
entries.snippet: A springbok Sorcerer, named !guerriten-ddé, was mentioned by /hanǂkass'o. !guerriten-dde has a cap made of the springbok's head. When he wears the cap he resembles a springbok.

entries.title: !gweh or Malkop Gift (poisons)
entries.snippet: Bushman poisons: their names given in Bushman, but the remarks respecting them in English only, after ǂkásin (L IV.-1. 3472-3480).

entries.title: !haken, a food resembling 'Bushman rice'
entries.snippet: !haken, a food resembling 'Bushman rice' is also used by Bushmen. (L VIII.-9. 6789 rev., note).

entries.title: !kaua doro and the lion
entries.snippet: [not translated]

entries.title: !kotta-kkoe, his brother, ostrich eggs and Korannas
entries.snippet: The two brothers, of the Early Race, who collected ostrich eggs, and were chased by Koranna-Hottentots. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.28. 8486-8506).

entries.title: !kuppen (imitation of the sound of horses)
entries.snippet: The admiration of Bushman women for the horse. The sound of the cantering of horses imitated by them. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-31. 8737-8740).

entries.title: !nana-an: the custom of calling to the wounded springbok
entries.snippet: The hunters call to make a wounded springbok die (or 'lie down') quickly (!nana-an). They call out differently for gemsbok and ostriches.

entries.title: !nanna-sse
entries.snippet: |hanǂkass'o's explanation of the term !nanna-sse: people put bones in a heap in a number of places. Different people take their finished bones and put them in the same place.

entries.title: !nanna-sse (hunting observances)
entries.snippet: About the hunting of game and the eating of flesh of certain game beforehand, such as eating slow-moving rather than fast game when going off to hunt.

entries.title: !nu !numma-!kuiten
entries.snippet: !nu!numma-!kuíten, who fetches crying children. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-29. 8555-8560).

entries.title: !nuin-ǀkui-ten (who was a sorcerer or magician)
entries.snippet: The sorcerer !nuin|kúïten. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-15. 5079-5101, 5104-5109). The sorcerer !nuin-|kui-ten becomes a lion and goes about just to look at people, not to hunt them.

entries.title: !xen and the steenbok
entries.snippet: !xen (Dirk) is afraid of the female steenbok, who has a baby steenbok, and throws dust in the steenbok's eyes to make it afraid of him (as he is of it).
entries.date_label: 4 November 1878 (started and finished on or about)

entries.title: !yoa-ka-ttu, the Blue Crane and the girls
entries.snippet: !yoa-ka-ttu, the Blue Crane, and the Girls of the early race. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-29. 8526-8554).

entries.title: A 'spelletje' or rhyme
entries.snippet: A |xam rhyme (not translated).

entries.title: A belief about the bat and the porcupine
entries.snippet: Habits of the Bat and the Porcupine, etc. By Día!kwãin, from his parents (L V.-6. 4378-4403).What Dia!kwain's parents told him about the bat and the porcupine who inhabit the same hole and go about together seeking…

entries.title: A Bushman belief about a moth called ǀgoro, the coming of which to the fire at night foretells the slaying of an ostrich (or the finding of ostrich eggs) by one of the men
entries.snippet: Successful Ostrich hunting foretold by the coming of a moth (Celœna renisigma, Walker). In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-18. 5390-5405).

entries.title: A discussion on the respective understanding and foolishnesses of various animals and some of their doings
entries.snippet: Some animals have' thoughts' (or 'understanding') and can talk as people do. According to ||kabbo (see p.891v), some animals stand upright like a man does. (Much of this story has not been translated.) ...

entries.title: A few words, names, etc., obtained from Blaitje Snell and Daoud Moos (chiefly the former); from Stuurman's, and Thier Fontein
entries.snippet: Names, words and sentences given by Blaitje Snell and Daoud Moos from Stuurman's and Thierfontein, the names of their relations and where they come from.

entries.title: A fragment about the animal clicks, and ways of speaking ǀxam
entries.snippet: A short account of the different animal clicks and ways of speaking |xam.

entries.title: A fragment of a story about Lions and Jackals
entries.snippet: A fable, somewhat similar to the Hottentot one of the lion and the jackal who went to shoot elands ('Reynard the Fox in South Africa', p.3, etc.), was told in the Katkop dialect, by ǂkásin (L IV.-1.

entries.title: A fragment of an account of a ceremony performed by Bushman maidens in order that their father's dogs should hunt well
entries.snippet: A fragment, regarding the ceremony to be performed by the maiden, was also given by Díä!kwãin (L V.-20. 5592 and 5593, translated, and 5591 rev., note).

entries.title: A fragment of the story of the early morning
entries.snippet: One person not to stand over another in the early morning. By ǂgirri-sse (L VII.-1. 6045 and 6046). A fragment of the account of what happens when one stands in the early morning.

entries.title: A further explanation of the ratel (or Mellivora): why it is like a man
entries.snippet: The vicious Water-tortoise and the charitable Ratel (Mellivora), a fable related to Dia!kwãin, by his mother (L V.-5. 4265-4291). The Ratel used to be a man.

entries.title: A girl does not eat porcupines' tails
entries.snippet: Different customs concerning the eating of the Porcupine. (L VII.-6. 6590 rev.-6594 rev.). Certain persons not allowed to eat the tail of the Porcupine (L VIII.-16. 7405-7409, 7408 rev. and 7409 rev.).

entries.title: A great Bushman doctress and sorceress Ttanno !khauken who did not understand Dutch
entries.snippet: More about the great sorceress Ttanno !kauken who did not understand Dutch: the parents taught the children to respect the great sorceress in many ways.

entries.title: A lion's story, or, The child who saved her sleeping parents from the lion
entries.snippet: A child is hungry and stays awake crying in the night while the parents sleep. A lion hears her and comes into the house.

entries.title: A man is wounded by another by accident when out after springbok
entries.snippet: A man shoots another accidentally on the hunting ground. He explains that dust from the springbok prevented him from seeing clearly and the wounded man confirms this.

entries.title: A mother's prohibitions with regard to the hartebeest and her child
entries.snippet: Some remarkable superstitions concerning the Hartebeest, whose head is said to resemble that of the Mantis; and also a curious charm made from the foot of the Hartebeest, and used by Bushman women for their children as…

entries.title: A necklace of reed used to cure a little child's cold
entries.snippet: A reed necklace employed as a remedy for a little child suffering from a cold (L VIII.-21. 7826 and 7827). The women break and burn pieces of reed in the fire.

entries.title: A note about the porcupine
entries.snippet: More about what Dia!kwain's parents instructed him about the porcupine; children should not eat the things that are at the back of the porcupine's neck.

entries.title: A note on the First Bushmen
entries.snippet: A series of three notes about the first people (the First Bushmen). In the first their children worked to raise the Sun; in the second they teach their children the histories of the first people; the third tells of the…

entries.title: A note to the story of The !kwai !kwai, the Mantis and the children
entries.snippet: The !kwái-!kwái, the Mantis, and the Children. The !kwái-!kwái, who was formerly a man, and is now a bird (in appearance somewhat resembling a 'duiker'), comes, during the absence of their parents, to kill the children.

entries.title: A person who takes snuff: tobacco eats up his brains
entries.snippet: The Bushman's opinion of snuff-taking. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-26. 8266 rev. and 8267 rev.). The |xam believe that a person who takes snuff has his brain eaten up by tobacco until it disappears.

entries.title: A photograph of Xu gwai reminding ǀhanǂkass'o of !nwa !koro
entries.snippet: Names of various Bushmen and Bushman women, with information regarding them. Partly in Bushman by and partly in English after |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-1. 6078-6081, 4. 6370 rev., note, 11. 6969 rev., note 14.

entries.title: A prayer addressed to the Moon, and another version of the Moon and Hare story
entries.snippet: A version of the Moon and Hare story (which treats of the Origin of Death), preceded by a prayer, addressed to the young Moon. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-15. 5159-5168 1/2, 16. 5169-5198).

entries.title: A presentiment
entries.snippet: Bushman Presentiments, etc. A feeling which tells us something that happens in another place. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-19. 5504 rev., note).

entries.title: A Rain story
entries.snippet: An account of rain-making by means of dragging a water-animal over the land, in illustration of a Bushman painting copied by Mr J.M.

entries.title: A review on the parade
entries.snippet: |hanǂkass'o describes a military parade (at the Grand Parade in Cape Town). The soldiers (British) are standing in rows.

entries.title: A root used for curing ill people
entries.bleek_lloyd_ref: not numbered (inserts inside back cover of A2_1_44)
entries.snippet: Descriptions and names of roots and plants used to cure a variety of ailments such as headaches.

entries.title: A small insect called !ka !karro, said to resemble the Moon, used by Bushmen women to ascertain if the men will bring food home
entries.snippet: A small insect called !ka !karro resembles and shares the Moon's name. The !ka !karro tells the truth because it 'possesses' game.

entries.title: A song sung by the star !gaunu, especially by the Bushman women
entries.snippet: Women sing this song of the Star !gaunu. It asks if the ||garraken flower is open and sings that the ǂku yam is the one that opens.

entries.title: A story about another Star
entries.snippet: Various statements are given with regard to the nature and movements of the celestial bodies. The first of these treats of the Moon and Stars, ending with the 'Bushman-rice' Star Canopus (B I. 291-294).

entries.title: A waterpool called ǀuha
entries.snippet: A waterpool is called |uha when there are many bushes growing there. The water's lightning is called ||ke-||kamminya and has caused the pool to appear this way.

entries.title: A white substance and the Moon
entries.snippet: A certain white substance found upon a bush, formerly said to proceed from the Moon. By /hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-23. 8040 rev., note).

entries.title: A wild cat, when many have been killed by a person changes itself into a lion and kills the slayer
entries.snippet: The Wild Cat believed to possess the power of turning itself into a lion. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-23. 8080-8083, and 27. 8399 rev., note).

entries.title: A yet unwritten story, about the Rain and one of the First Bushmen girls carried away by a whirlwind and who became a frog
entries.snippet: A note has been made, from ǁkábbo's information, of a story not yet written down in Bushman, telling how the Rain carried off a girl belonging to the First Bushmen (or the ancient people living before the Bushmen), who…

entries.title: A young woman of the Early Race of people carried off by the Rain, in the shape of a bull
entries.snippet: A young woman of the Early Race is carried off by the Rain in the form of a bull. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-16. 7434-7448). The Rain courted a young woman of the Early Race, because of her scent.

entries.title: About 'Bushman rice'
entries.snippet: Bushman rice' is live ant larvae. Women prepare it in the heat and dry it on a mat and sift the fat off.

entries.title: About a moth called by the Bushmen !num-!num and said to pour lice upon them
entries.snippet: The !num !num moth (or Aloa amasis) is said by the |xam to pour lice upon them when it hovers above. This story describes what the people say when the moth does this and how they drive it away.

entries.title: About cattle
entries.snippet: Cattle drink at the water and eat grass, and then sleep when they are full.

entries.title: About Dia!kwain's relations
entries.snippet: Names and information about Dia!kwain's brothers and sisters.

entries.title: About locusts
entries.snippet: When a swarm of locusts comes flying past the people must go to the place where the locusts settle and make fire. The rainmaker, |kannu, grew locusts and gave them to the people.

entries.title: About maidens and how they adorn young men with ǁka or 'rooi klip'
entries.snippet: What a Maiden must eat and avoid (L II.-28. 2520-2524). How a New Maiden is treated, has been told in the Katkop dialect, by !kwéiten ta ǁken (L VI.-2.

entries.title: About new maidens (continued from Bleek's Book XXVII: p.2618)
entries.snippet: A note has been made, from ||kábbo's information, of a story not yet written down in Bushman, telling how the Rain carried off a girl belonging to the First Bushmen (or the ancient people living before the Bushmen), who…

entries.title: About poison
entries.snippet: The !gwé plant, from which poison is derived. (L VIII.-7. 6603 rev.).

entries.title: About snoring
entries.snippet: A note on the 'snoring' of sick people and the 'sneezing out' of sickness by the healer after doing so.

entries.title: About sorcerers
entries.snippet: The Bushman Doctor or Sorcerer (L II.-1. 273-275). An account of these people was also given in the Katkop dialect by Dia!kwáin (L V.-3. 4122-4131), from whom we besides have two reports of cures performed upon himself.

entries.title: About sorcerers: their death, their snoring work, earthquakes and the rain
entries.snippet: Further details concerning what occurs at the death of a sorcerer, and concerning the harmful and beneficial doings of sorcerers (L V.-19. 5506-5530). When a sorcerer dies, an earthquake takes place, and a star shoots.

entries.title: About Ssho ǀoa: where to be found
entries.snippet: The Shó-|õä in general use is not found upon the flats, but in the mountains near the Orange River, and in the mountains and rivers, generally speaking, throughout the country.

entries.title: About the 'Toornan': the 'Bushman witchdoctor'
entries.snippet: The Bushman Doctor or Sorcerer (L II.-1. 273-275). An account of these people was also given in the Katkop dialect by Dia!kwáin (L V.-3. 4122-4131).

entries.title: About the Daybreak Star
entries.snippet: About the Daybreak (Day's Heart or Dawn's Heart) Star which has a heart (that is red and 'flesh'). The Daybreak Star has a Daybreak Star Heart child when it is an old Daybreak Star Heart.

entries.title: About the fine hearing of the porcupine
entries.snippet: Habits of the Bat and the Porcupine, etc. By Día!kwãin, from his parents (L V.-6. 4378-4403).

entries.title: About the hartebeest
entries.snippet: The hartebeest stands fast and is a 'wild thing'.

entries.title: About the hyena and its doings
entries.snippet: Habits of the Hyena (L II.-9. 921-927, 928-930). The hyena carries off dead men and puts them in its hole for it and its family to eat, and goes to drink when the sun has set.

entries.title: About the jackal and its doings
entries.snippet: The jackal hunts mice in the afternoon.

entries.title: About the korhaan
entries.snippet: (Not translated.) The Knorhaan (L II-9. 934 and 935).

entries.title: About the lion who carries away and kills a man and the search for the lost man (a story of common life)
entries.snippet: A Bushman killed by a Lion; a search for the missing man; removal of the rest to another place, for fear that the Lion should track them to their huts (L II.-8. 892-907, 9. 908-920).

entries.title: About the Mantis
entries.snippet: |kaggen (the Mantis) and his wife (the Dassie) and what people call her. [Most of this story has not been translated.] The originality of all the preceding myths [about |kaggen], and the impossibility of their owing…

entries.title: About the sorceress !kwarra-an
entries.snippet: The Bushman Doctor or Sorcerer (L II.-1. 273-275). An account of these people was also given in the Katkop dialect by Dia!kwáin (L V.-3. 4122-4131), from whom we besides have two reports of cures performed upon himself.

entries.title: About the wildebeest
entries.snippet: The Gnu or Wildebeest (L II.-9. 933 and 934.)

entries.title: Address or prayer to the star Canopus and the star Sirius
entries.snippet: Bushman doings and prayers when Canopus and his grandmother Sirius come out (L II.-37. 3348-3355). An address or prayer to the stars Canopus and Sirius.

entries.title: Amsterdam Battery, near Cape Town: July 15/79
entries.snippet: Name, etc., of a Bushman at the Amsterdam Battery, in 1879 (L VII.-1. 6046g). Words. Given by Xu gwai (L VII.-1. 6046g-6046j). Also known as Jantje George.

entries.title: An account of the rain's things or !khoa ka ǁkerri-ssi !kau
entries.snippet: The rain's 'things' (or !khoa ka ||kerri-ssi !kau) of |hanǂkass'o's country have large bodies and dwell in water; some of them kill people when they bite them.

entries.title: An eruptive illness called !hamman-xu
entries.snippet: An illness which scratches and affects the throat which does not cause death.

entries.title: An ignorant man digs up Ssho ǀoa and the consequences of his actions
entries.snippet: An ignorant man having gone to dig up Shó-!õä, is discovered speechless and motionless, sitting among serpents, by the hole where he had been digging.

entries.title: An illustration of the use of ddabba-i
entries.snippet: The term ddabba-i is used when parents educate their children so that they have understanding and do not do 'foolish actions'.

entries.title: An ostrich eggshell, left open, will attract snakes
entries.snippet: The mouth of an Ostrich egg-shell not to be left open. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-25. 8234 rev. and 8235 rev.).

entries.title: An owl believed to foretell the coming of the lion
entries.snippet: The coming of an owl is considered as a sign of the approach of a Lion. An insult offered by a little child, to a fly, is reported by the latter to the Lion, as disrespect shown to himself.

entries.title: Aquilae's water
entries.snippet: A note regarding the latter stars [named by !gãunu] was given by |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-13. 7119 rev.). About a rain called 'Aquilae's water' and about rain-clouds which resemble celestial bodies.

entries.title: Arrow-making
entries.snippet: Arrow making, and Arrow poisoning. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-26. 8293-8302, 8315-8334). Two kinds of Arrow. (L VIII.-10. 6923 rev. and 6924 rev., note). Arrow heads, etc. (L VIII.-1. 6086 and 6087).
entries.date_label: 2 March (and taken up again on the 16 March) - 24 March 1879

entries.title: Arrowheads
entries.snippet: Arrow making, and Arrow poisoning. (L VIII.-26. 8293-8302, 8315-8334). Two kinds of Arrow. (L VIII.-10. 6923 rev. and 6924 rev., note). Arrow heads, etc. (L VIII.-1. 6086 and 6087).

entries.title: Avoidance behaviour relating to lions
entries.snippet: The Lion. Power over the time of sunset, and over the water-skins of the Bushmen, possessed by him. Flies tell him that a Bushman intends to fetch water, to-day. Caution in fetching water is recommended.

entries.title: Avoiding where the jackal or the hyena have passed water
entries.snippet: Certain spots, where the jackal, or the hyena, has been, avoided by Bushmen, for fear of skin disease. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.29. 8584 rev.-8587 rev.).

entries.title: Baboon's ss'o ǀa and hair used as charms against illness
entries.snippet: ...further described the value attached, by the Bushmen, to Ss'ó/õä found in the possession of Baboons; also its great use to the latter. The hair of the Baboon considered as a charm (L V.-24. 5957-5967).

entries.title: Baboons and quagga are people
entries.snippet: About the Baboons and the Quagga, who were formerly people when those who are now people were not alive. Even though they are animals their organs still smell like they did when they were people.

entries.title: Baboons and the ǂgebbi-ggu
entries.snippet: The Bushmen are said to have learnt the ǂgebbi-ggu from the Baboons, by whom it is still believed to be played.

entries.title: Baboons and ǁxabbiten ǁxabbiten
entries.snippet: Baboons plan to attack ||xabbiten ||xabbiten. ||xabbiten ||xabbiten asks them what they are saying and then makes remarks about their ugly appearance and climbs a Krieboom tree.

entries.title: Baboons dance the ǂgebbi-ggu
entries.snippet: The Baboons are also said to assemble together and play at the ǂgebbi-ggu, at night, like the Bushmen, singing like the Bushman women, and imitating the songs which they have heard the people sing. (L V.-25. 5998-6005).

entries.title: Baboons know our names
entries.snippet: The name of a Bushman seems to be known to a Baboon, even when the latter beholds him for the first time. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-24. 5927-5929).

entries.title: Baboons should not be spoken with
entries.snippet: Baboons not to be answered, when they address a Bushman in the early morning on his way to the hunting ground.

entries.title: Baboons speak Bushman, and have wives
entries.snippet: Baboons are said to speak the Bushman language and to possess wives. Their use of Ss'ó|õä, which informs them of matters otherwise unknown to them. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-24.

entries.title: Baboons try to shoot people - means of preventing It
entries.snippet: When shot, baboons will often try and throw the arrow back at the hunter and kill him too.

entries.title: Baboons who ate human beings
entries.snippet: In former times, when animals were people, the Baboons were jealous of a young Quagga woman, who lived with them, on account of the notice attracted by her great size.

entries.title: Beasts of prey were once people
entries.snippet: All things were once people. They became beasts of prey when the Anteater and the Lynx cursed each other.

entries.title: Birds await the death of a thing
entries.snippet: A note describing how birds (and specifically crows) await the death of a thing by sitting opposite it.

entries.title: Butterflies and !giten
entries.snippet: Illness, for which the Doctor is called in, believed to be caused by butterflies. The narrator's personal experience of this. Bushman children not allowed to throw stones at butterflies. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-20.

entries.title: Certain kinds of food, said not to be eaten by adults
entries.snippet: Certain kinds of food not eaten by adults. By lhanǂkass'o (L VIII.-30. 8649 and 8650). The |xam do not eat pigs, fish, little birds and many kinds of mice.

entries.title: Children do not eat jackal's hearts
entries.snippet: Little children, among the Bushmen, are not allowed to eat the heart of the Jackal, on account of its great timidity. The heart of the Leopard may, on the contrary, be eaten by them. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII-16.

entries.title: Children do not say the lion's name at night
entries.snippet: The children call the lion different names by day and by night and wish to avoid insulting (or slighting it).

entries.title: Chippings made by Dia!kwain's father (before the time of the 'Boers')
entries.snippet: Chippings of Gemsbok, Quagga, Ostriches, etc., executed by Xãä-ttin (father of the informant), to be found at the place (not yet identified) called !kaññ, where these animals used formerly to come to drink.

entries.title: Clouds and wind
entries.snippet: Some beliefs of the Bushmen with regard to clouds and wind, etc. (L VIII.-18. 7589-7592).

entries.title: Concerning apparitions (or How, when the first wife of Dia!kwain was buried, those returning to their homes saw the apparition of a little child)
entries.snippet: Apparition seen by the party returning from the burial of the narrator's wife; followed by the account of an apparition seen, on another occasion, by the narrator's brother-in-law.

entries.title: Concerning different Bushmen
entries.snippet: Names of various Bushmen and Bushman women, with information regarding them. Partly in Bushman by and partly in English after |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-1. 6078-6081, 4. 6370 rev., note, 11. 6969 rev., note 14.

entries.title: Concerning places and topographical features in ǀhanǂkass'o's country; his father-in-law's place is ǁgubbo
entries.snippet: Explanation of the Bushman name for a certain rock, with a description of its situation. (L VIII.-20. 7751 and 7752). |hanǂkass'o describes different features of, and places in, his country.

entries.title: Concerning the ǀkuken-ǀu ǀunu, which is found abundantly, in Bushmanland
entries.snippet: A note about the caterpillar |kuken-|u |unu (caterpillar larva of Aloa) in which its appearance and habits are described, including its other name ('little fine hair') and why people fear it.

entries.title: Crows and a note on secretary birds
entries.snippet: Another version of the story of the Crow Messengers appears, here, in connexion with the adventure related above [The Mantis and |kwammana visit the Dassie's house]; /kwámmana and his companion being, in this instance,…

entries.title: Cursing
entries.snippet: Various modes of cursing in use among the Bushmen. By |hanǂkass'o (L.VIII.-31. 8741-8743).

entries.title: Custom observed with Bushman children, when too young to walk
entries.snippet: A ceremony performed with the Brachycerus (African Ground Weevil) in order to preserve young babies from convulsions. In the Katkop dialect by Díä!kwãin (L V.-23. 5833-5840).

entries.title: Cuts to be made on a bow when a baboon has been killed
entries.snippet: Certain cuts to be made upon the bow, when a Baboon has been killed. A similar ceremony to be observed with regard to the Hyena. A Baboon, when killed, resembles a man, in 'making cloud'.

entries.title: Cuts to be made on a bow when a hyena has been killed
entries.snippet: Certain cuts to be made upon the bow, when a Baboon has been killed. A similar ceremony to be observed with regard to the Hyena. A Baboon, when killed, resembles a man, in 'making cloud'.

entries.title: Cutting with stone knives
entries.snippet: Making knives out of different types of stone, which are used for cutting.

entries.title: Ddi xerreten and the Lioness
entries.snippet: The Man of the Early Race, whose head was of stone, the Lioness, and the Children. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-25. 8177-8197).

entries.title: Dead people are those who rode the Rain
entries.snippet: The Rain believed to be ridden by sorcerers. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-27. 8399 rev. and 8400 rev., note).

entries.title: Description of animals and their habits
entries.snippet: A description of the appearance, habits and homes of various animals. Included are more lengthy narratives about the korhaan malkop (who sits on her eggs), the lion (who springs out of a bush and catches a man), and the…

entries.title: Description of the ǂnerru
entries.snippet: A description of the ǂnerru bird (in contemporary times).

entries.title: Destroying the sneeze or kkoroken
entries.snippet: Sneezing in the early morning considered to be unfortunate. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V. 21. 5654 rev. and 5655 rev., note). How to destroy a Sneeze. (L V.-22. 5658 rev., note).

entries.title: Details about various people known by ǀhanǂkass'o
entries.snippet: Names of various Bushmen and Bushman women, with information regarding them. Partly in Bushman by and partly in English after |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-1. 6078-6081, 4. 6370 rev., note, 11. 6969 rev., note 14.

entries.title: Dia!kwain explains his mother's 'little name'
entries.snippet: Explanation of various personal names was given, in the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-10. 4751-4754, 4758-4777, 18. 5373 rev., 19. 5505 rev. and 5506 rev., 20. 5572 and 5573, 5605-5607).

entries.title: Dia!kwain plays the bow in a thunderstorm, or, The thunderstorm
entries.snippet: An account by Dia!kwain of the time that he played the !goura while the rain fell and caused lightning at night.

entries.title: Dia!kwain's explanation of the name which his mother gave him
entries.snippet: Explanation of various personal names was given, in the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-10. 4751-4754, 4758-4777, 18. 5373 rev., 19. 5505 rev. and 5506 rev., 20. 5572 and 5573, 5605-5607).

entries.title: Difference between ǀxam and European methods of articulation
entries.snippet: The |xam talk with the body of their tongue, whereas the Europeans speak with the tip of their tongue.

entries.title: Digging for porcupine
entries.snippet: When the people go digging for porcupine they pluck off its quills at its hole and place them neatly ('nicely') together so that the wind will not disperse them.

entries.title: Digging sticks used by men
entries.snippet: The digging-sticks used by men are not weighted with stones. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-23. 8083 rev., note).

entries.title: Dirk (!xein, son of Dootje)
entries.snippet: Information regarding narrator's family. In English after and in Bushman by |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-1. 6052 and 6053, 19. 7671, 26. 8302 rev.).

entries.title: Distribution of porcupine meat
entries.snippet: Various parts of the porcupine are given to specific people in the group, for instance the wife of the man who killed the porcupine eats its tail. The hunter and the children eat its shoulder meat.

entries.title: Doings of the Mantis when the eland has been wounded
entries.snippet: An account of the doings of the Mantis when an Eland has been wounded. The protection afforded by the Mantis to the Eland is also alluded by Díä!kwãin, in 'Rules to be observed when an Eland has been shot, etc.' (L…

entries.title: Doings of the springbok and springbok hunting
entries.snippet: The behaviour of male and female springbok and various hunting strategies.

entries.title: Dreams and rain
entries.snippet: ||kunnu and other old men dream of rain, which speedily comes. (L VIII.-23. 8005-8010).

entries.title: Dust signals, or, A man who becomes faint from the heat of the sun on his way home, throws up earth into the air, so that those at home may see the dust, and come to help him
entries.snippet: A Bushman, who becomes faint from the heat of the sun on his way home, throws earth into the air, so that those who are home may see the dust, and come to help him. (L VIII.-22. 7961-7969).

entries.title: Earthquake
entries.snippet: How to relieve a little child from the alarm caused by the sound of an earthquake. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-22. 5728-5735). Earthquakes happen at the time when sorcerers seek people to take away.

entries.title: Eating hare's meat
entries.snippet: A certain small portion of the flesh of the Hare is refrained from by the Bushmen. In English, after |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.27. 8398 rev.).

entries.title: Eating the springbok's tongue-tip
entries.snippet: The tip of the tongue of the Springbok is not eaten by children. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-25. 6025-6030).

entries.title: Edible plants found near water
entries.snippet: A description of two edible plants found near water in Bushmanland.

entries.title: Explanation of !hau-!hau (a hunting charm)
entries.snippet: A man, who has missed his aim in shooting, throws springbok bones, in order that another man may do likewise. (L VIII.-11. 7010 rev., note).

entries.title: Explanation of Mr G. Stow's pictures: 1 (habits of the hunting leopard)
entries.snippet: Dia!kwain comments on George Stow's copy of a Bushman painting (no. 1) which features figures and various animals (numbered). He describes the doings of the |xam with regard to hunting.

entries.title: Explanation of Mr G. Stow's pictures: no. 2
entries.snippet: An explanation of No. 2 of Mr G.W. Stow's collection of copies of Bushman Paintings was given, in the Katkop dialect by Díä!kwãin (L V.-22. 5739-5742). The painting features figures (numbered) and animals.

entries.title: Extract from 'Russian Folk Tales' by W.R.S Ralston; pp.111-113: 'Something which reminds me in a degree of the Bushmen's expressions about the heart falling down'; referring to an Egyptian tale
entries.snippet: An extract about an Egyptian folktale, taken from a book of Russian Folktales by Ralston, which reminded Lloyd of the |xam's 'heart-falling-down' stories.

entries.title: Falling stars
entries.snippet: Falling Stars. Certain ceremonies in connection with them. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-19. 5478-5483, 5481 rev.,-5483 rev., note).

entries.title: Falling stars
entries.snippet: The human heart is believed to fall down at death. A star likewise falls. (L V.-22. 5731 rev.-5733 rev., note).

entries.title: Feathered arrows and poison
entries.snippet: Arrow making, and Arrow poisoning. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-26. 8293-8302, 8315-8334). Two kinds of Arrow. (L VIII.-10. 6923 rev. and 6924 rev., note). Arrow heads, etc. (L VIII.-1. 6086 and 6087).

entries.title: Flat Bushmen's' poison
entries.snippet: The |xam smell the poison which is warm and burns their nostrils. 'Grass Bushmen's' poison is a golden colour, 'Flat Bushmen's' poison is from a thorny bush or tree with a milky juice, and 'Flat Bushmen' also use snake…

entries.title: Flood at Victoria West
entries.snippet: |hanǂkass'o relates the story of the flood at Victoria West, observed by ǂkuken-an and |hanǂkass'o's wife, Ssuobba-||ken (Sara), who were both at Victoria West at the time it happened.

entries.title: Food eaten by Bushmen (five types of berries and roots)
entries.snippet: Certain kinds of Food, used by Bushmen. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-1. 6107, 18. 7598-7601). An account of the food (plants and berries) eaten by people throughout the year.

entries.title: Food of the Bushmen, found in their country
entries.snippet: Certain kinds of Food, used by Bushmen. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-1. 6107, 18. 7598-7601).

entries.title: Food that ǂkasin says he does not eat
entries.snippet: ǂkasin does not eat certain foods such as fish, crabs, fowls or pigs; his father does eat fish, but his mother does not.

entries.title: Fragment of a story about the old man, the little Hare and the travelling Lions
entries.snippet: The Old Man, the Little Hare, and the Lions. Related by ǂgiri-sse (L VII.-1. 6032-6040, partly translated). A fragment of the above story was also related by ǂgiri-sse (L VII.-1. 6031).

entries.title: Fragment of the story of the old woman sending the children to throw up the sleeping Sun into the sky (given by Blaitje Snell)
entries.snippet: The Sun, a man from whose armpit brightness proceeded, lived formerly on earth; but only gave light for a space around his house.

entries.title: From 'Jan Plat' at Charlton House, Mowbray
entries.snippet: Some words and sentences given by Jan Plat at Charlton House in Mowbray. The contribution includes some personal details, such the names of some of his relations.

entries.title: Further adventures of the Mantis and !goe !kweitentu
entries.snippet: Another story of the fight between |kaggen (the Mantis) and !goe !kweitentu (first taken down in Bleek's notebooks), in which |kaggen is defeated.

entries.title: Further adventures of the Mantis and the Cat
entries.snippet: |kaggen (the Mantis) fights the Cat and is beaten because he fights the Cat's tail instead of its head. He gets feathers and flies into the water, followed by his things (the Hartebeest children).

entries.title: Further ceremonies in cutting up eland
entries.snippet: More ceremonies performed when cutting up eland, as it is a 'magic thing'.

entries.title: Further details of Men who hunted lions with bones
entries.snippet: (Further details of the story of the men who hunted with bones.) The elder brother waits at home for his younger brother to return from hunting Lions.

entries.title: Gambro or ǀkui and its ill-effects
entries.snippet: The ill effects of eating !kùï ( . . . . ) were mentioned by |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.7. 6679 rev., note). A note describing the ill-effects of gambro, or |kui.

entries.title: Game once tame: why it grew wild
entries.snippet: Dia!kwain's grandfather (!xugen-ddi) told him that game was once tame. The game acted as oxen do and allowed the people to stroke them, but a person called Chaser-of-food was in the habit of stroking the Quagga and then…

entries.title: Gargling
entries.snippet: Gargling forbidden by the Bushman mothers, for fear of evil consequences. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-20. 5569 rev. and 5570 rev.).

entries.title: Gemsbok hunting
entries.snippet: A partly translated story about a man hunting gemsbok, sees a transformed lion and steps on his poisoned arrow, which pierces his foot, poisoning him.

entries.title: Genealogical information concerning the family of ǀhanǂkass'o (or Klein Jantje)
entries.snippet: Information regarding narrator's family. In English after and in Bushman by /hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-1. 6052 and 6053, 19. 7671, 26. 8302 rev.).

entries.title: Genealogies
entries.snippet: A good many genealogical notes and portions of Bushman family history have been taken down (B II. 349, 359 rev., 360-362, 364, 365 368-373, 376; BXXV. 2352, 2412 and 2416; L III.-1. 476-506; L IV.-1.

entries.title: Genealogies: Ttono wo (who was killed by a rhinoceros)
entries.snippet: ||kabbo's maternal grandfather Ttono wo was killed by an adult rhinoceros. The names of some of Ttono wo's children are listed, with details of their personal history.

entries.title: Grass in Bushmanland that resembles brushes
entries.snippet: A note describing different types of grasses found in Bushmanland, as well as various kinds of brushes (used for wiping the face).

entries.title: Greetings among the Bushmen (and times of the day)
entries.snippet: Forms of Salutation used by Bushmen. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-32. 8853-8857, 8852 rev.). Men and women as well as the young and the old greet in different ways.

entries.title: Habits of jackals
entries.snippet: An exhortation, to the Bushmen, to be careful of food in time of plenty, is followed by a description of the jackal's doings with regard to food, etc. (L VIII.-16. 7449-7451, 7448 rev.-7450 rev., and 7452-7456).

entries.title: Habits of porcupines
entries.snippet: Different customs concerning the eating of the Porcupine. (L VII.-6. 6590 rev.-6594 rev.). Certain persons not allowed to eat the tail of the Porcupine. (L VIII.-16. 7405-7409, 7408 rev. and 7409 rev.).

entries.title: Healers (sorcerers) and the ǁken dance
entries.snippet: The Bushman Doctor or Sorcerer (who may be of either sex.) By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-20. 7757-7762, 7768-7774). Remarks upon sorcerers, their dress, etc.

entries.title: Hendrik (Ronebout) at Breakwater hospital
entries.snippet: Names of three Bushmen and another Native, who were at the Breakwater Convict Station in 1875, together with a little information regarding them. Taken down in English (L V.-19. 5445-5447, 5453-5456).

entries.title: Horns burnt for rain
entries.snippet: Horns to be burnt, when the weather looks very threatening. (L VIII.-23. 8030 and 8031).

entries.title: How 'Mansse' obtained the name of !kau ǁhoan
entries.snippet: Explanation of various personal names was given, in the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-10. 4751-4754, 4758-4777, 18. 5373 rev., 19. 5505 rev. and 5506 rev., 20. 5572 and 5573, 5605-5607).

entries.title: How a lion carried off ǂkasin's eldest brother and wounded his father
entries.snippet: How a Lion carried off the narrator's eldest brother, and wounded his father. In the Katkop dialect, by ǂkásin (L IV.-1. 3453-3458).

entries.title: How an old woman asked a chameleon for rain
entries.snippet: How an old woman asked a Chameleon for rain, and knew, from its way of looking, that rain would fall; as it indeed did that very night. In the Katkop dialect, by ǂkásin, who heard it from his mother (L IV.-3.

entries.title: How an owl (by its conduct) made ǀa-kkumm think that danger must be at hand and how she was sought for by a lion, which spoke to her in a man's voice
entries.snippet: Dia!kwain's elder sister, |a-kkumm, comes upon an owl as she journeys from !kau-|nunu (Kenhardt) at sunset, and it behaves as if it is going to snatch her.

entries.title: How children are carried
entries.snippet: Manner of carrying a young baby in use among Bushman women. Partly in Bushman by and partly in English after |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.27. 8406 rev., note).

entries.title: How Korannas and the ǀxam cut themselves in order to shoot well
entries.snippet: Korannas cut themselves in order to shoot well and cut the back of their right wrists when they fight with their fists so that they may strike a man dead.

entries.title: How sorcerers sometimes assume the form of a jackal or of a bird; how they then act
entries.snippet: The form of a jackal, or of a little bird, is sometimes assumed by friendly sorcerers.In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-14. 5055-5078 1/2).

entries.title: How the !gabbaken-!gabbaken (the Mason Wasp) punished his wife for making personal remarks
entries.snippet: The Mason Wasp (? Genus Lyrops) who shot his wife for making a personal remark. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-12. 7098-7103).

entries.title: How the approach of a commando is foretold by the mist
entries.snippet: The near approach of a 'Commando' is heralded by a mist. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-16. 5199-5205).

entries.title: How the Blue Crane vainly sought for !kuommain ǀka ǁkau, was killed and eaten by the Lions, and restored to life by means of one of the bones of the Mantis (Part 2)
entries.snippet: The Blue Crane, the two Lions, and the Mantis. In this story, the Blue Crane, while searching for the Frog's husband, is killed and eaten by the Lions, and restored to life by the Mantis. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-32.

entries.title: How the brother of the maiden taken up in a whirlwind became the Porcupine; followed by various remarks about porcupines
entries.snippet: The Young man (of the early race) who was changed into a porcupine. He is said to be the brother of the girl in the preceding legend. Remarks regarding the porcupine follow. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-13.

entries.title: How the Bushman women show their admiration for the horse
entries.snippet: The admiration of Bushman women for the horse. The sound of the cantering of horses imitated by them. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-31. 8737-8740).

entries.title: How the Ichneumon discovered what the Mantis did with the honey
entries.snippet: In the arrangement of the myths regarding the Mantis, it has appeared to me most convenient to place first that one in which the Mantis takes away a shoe belonging to his son-in-law /kwámmana, and converts it into an…

entries.title: How the Ichneumon discovered what the Mantis did with the honey (a second 'right' version)
entries.snippet: A second 'right' version (or elaboration) of How the Ichneumon discovered what the Mantis did with the honey,which Dia!kwain tells to Lloyd when going over the first version in February 1874.

entries.title: How the Jackal deceived the Hyena
entries.snippet: The 'Jackal's Tower' still visible as a 'spitzkop' on the other side of the Zak River gives rise to a 'Jackal and Hyena' fable, told to Dia!kwãin by his mother ǂkámme-an (L V.-4. 4231-4252 1/2).

entries.title: How the prepared springbok's ears are tied on
entries.snippet: see The Bushman drum, and dancing-rattles. Mode of preparation of the latter, by the women. By |hanǂkass'o, (L VIII.-1. 6127-6137).

entries.title: How to get rid of the evil influence of bad dreams
entries.snippet: Mode of getting rid of the evil influence of bad dreams. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-15. 5160 rev.-5163 rev.).

entries.title: How various mice make their nests
entries.snippet: How various kinds of mice make their nests in Bushmanland.

entries.title: How women fear the new Ssho ǀoa which has just been brought home
entries.snippet: Newly-brought Shó-|õä is feared by the women (L II.-36. 3279-3286).

entries.title: How Xaa-ttin asked (or prayed to) the dead magician (named !nuin-ǀkui-ten) for rain, which was speedily bestowed
entries.snippet: A dead Sorcerer, name !nuin|kúïten, asked by the narrator's father for rain; which speedily came. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-14. 5068rev.-5078-rev.).

entries.title: How ǀhanǂkass'o's pet leveret was killed
entries.snippet: |hanǂkass'o's mother sends him to collect water and kills the leveret (hare) while he is away. |hanǂkass'o' is upset and his mother tells him a story (The death of the !kau) to soothe him.

entries.title: Hunting animals with dogs
entries.snippet: Hunting the 'Löffelhund' (Proteles) and Jackal. (L II.-1. 217 and 218.) The man hunts various animals with a dog.

entries.title: Hunting hare
entries.snippet: The hunting of hare, and its habits.

entries.title: Hunting hares
entries.snippet: A description of the shooting and killing of hares. The man carries the hare by its feet, breaks its arms and legs with a stick, and takes it home to his wife in a sack.

entries.title: Inserts in Lloyd's Book II-36
Jackal clouds
entries.snippet: Clouds. Preparations to be made for the coming rain; the springbok to be watched for, etc. (L VIII.-23. 8018-8029). Jackal clouds are rain-clouds and are black.

entries.title: Jan Plat (at Breakwater; looked over later at Mowbray)
entries.snippet: Names of three Bushmen and another Native, who were at the Breakwater Convict Station in 1875, together with a little information regarding them. Taken down in English (L V.-19. 5445-5447, 5453-5456).

entries.title: Jan Plat's aunt Natta sang the ǂgebbi-ggu
entries.snippet: Jan Plat's aunt Natta sang and taught the people the singing of the ǂgebbi-ggu. She could imitate (by singing) a ewe's call to its young as well as the call of a partridge, using different parts of her…

entries.title: Jan Plat's story about his brother Ruyter
entries.snippet: Information regarding the family of 'Jan Plat' (a mixed Namaqua Hottentot and Bushman) was taken down, in English, after Díä!kwãin (L V.-23. 5872, 5871 rev. and 6872 rev., 25. 6006 rev., note).

entries.title: Jan Ronebout or ǂgerri-sse (at Breakwater and later at Mowbray)
entries.snippet: Names of three Bushmen and another Native, who were at the Breakwater Convict Station in 1875, together with a little information regarding them. Taken down in English (L V.-19.

entries.title: Jantje (ǁkabbo) at museum. May 30 /71 (names of animals)
entries.snippet: ||kabbo's list of the |xam names for animals, given at the South African Museum, with some descriptions.

entries.title: Jantje Tooren tells me his dream
entries.snippet: ||kabbo's 'Dream of lions which talked', and also of his wife !kwábba-an, who asked him why he had not yet returned to her. This followed by a description of his and |a!kúnta's morning work (L ll.-22. 1949-1964).

entries.title: Jantje Tooren told by his father not to look towards the Moon as it comes out
entries.snippet: A Bushman child warned by its father not to look at the Moon as it rises behind the Mountain, for fear of arousing its anger and causing it to become obscured (L II.-35. 3157 rev.).

entries.title: Jantje Tooren's asking for thread to sew on his buttons that I gave him
entries.snippet: ||kabbo asks for thread to sew buttons given to him by Lucy Lloyd onto his jacket.

entries.title: Jewellery and ornaments worn by women
entries.snippet: Notes on these verso pages describe articles of jewellery and ornaments worn by women.

entries.title: Karosses must not be beaten upon the ground, for fear of bad consequences
entries.snippet: What the Grass Bushmen do when angry. Karosses not to be beaten upon the ground, for fear of causing illness, and for fear of sorcerers. A way in which the latter cause illness is here mentioned.

entries.title: Kinds of rain
entries.snippet: Different kinds of rain. The children to hide from rain that is likely to be strong, for fear of the lightning. (L VIII.-9. 6813 rev.-6815 rev.).

entries.title: Kki-a-ǁken who threw stones at swallows
entries.snippet: Regarding various Bushmen. Partly in English after and partly in Bushman (L V.-21. 5702 rev. and 5703 rev.). A note about the child Kki-a-||ken (who had been made very ill after throwing stones at a swallow).

entries.title: Klaas Paai or ǂenn (at Breakwater)
entries.snippet: Names of three Bushmen and another Native, who were at the Breakwater Convict Station in 1875, together with a little information regarding them. Taken down in English (L V.-19. 5445-5447, 5453-5456).

entries.title: Koranna commando destroyed by ǁua
entries.snippet: The ||ua (Fox) speaks, saying 'Thou art thinking thou art awake' and makes the Koranna sleep, even though they are supposed to be keeping watch.

entries.title: Koranna words with ǀxam and English translations
entries.snippet: Koranna-Hottentot Words, with their Bushman and English translations from ǂkásin (B XXV. 2365-2373, 2375-2413; L IV.-1.

entries.title: Leopard hunting: the fatal adventure of !kwai-!kwa and his companion, and advice about leopard-hunting
entries.snippet: A man called Gemsbok Leg, or !kwai-!kwa, goes hunting with a companion and they pursue a leopard that seems to disappear, but it has deceived them and is lying in wait.

entries.title: Life after death
entries.snippet: The names of those who are dead must not be uttered by the children at night. (L VIII.-26. 8310-8312). According to |hanǂkass'o's grandfather (Tsatsi), when people die they become spirit-people.

entries.title: Lightning which is black, it is that which kills us
entries.snippet: Black lightning kills people. It comes up on them without them seeing it.

entries.title: Lion and giraffe
entries.snippet: Lions and Giraffes, in two versions. (B1. 323-323; L II-1. 255-257) The lion catches the giraffe and leaps on its neck. He springs down because it is very far and falls to the ground and dies.

entries.title: Lion eats all things
entries.snippet: An account of the doings of a family of lions (apparently a continuation of one of the Ichneumon's speeches, in the myth of the Mantis) gives a good deal of conversation of such a description as might naturally be…

entries.title: Lion turns into a man
entries.snippet: The Lion can transform itself into a man. (L VIII-18. 7630 rev., note). The lion puts its tail over its head and trots along as a man does, because it feels that it resembles a real man.

entries.title: Lions
entries.snippet: Lions (who are different) steal to the people's houses at night and carry them away while they sleep. The people burn the lions out of the reeds and chase them away.

entries.title: Locust birds
entries.snippet: Locust birds not to be molested; etc. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-31. 8763-8765, 8763 rev. and 8764 rev.).

entries.title: Locusts
entries.snippet: About the locust as well as the bird that eats it.

entries.title: Locusts
entries.snippet: |kaunu, the rainmaker, told the people that locusts come out when rain falls. After locusts have laid their eggs they fly away. New locusts get wings and become berre.

entries.title: Locusts or ǁkabba-ǀkha
entries.snippet: A note on the locust and its names, proper and |xam, as well as its appearance.

entries.title: Making fire with two pieces of sticks; and tinder-making
entries.snippet: Making fire with two pieces of stick. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII. -28. 8471 and 8472). The people make fire (when they have no tinderbox) using two pieces of stick (from a divided arrow).

entries.title: Manner of carrying firewood
entries.snippet: The manner of carrying firewood. By|hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-15. 7360 rev. and 7361 rev., note).

entries.title: Mat sieves
entries.snippet: Mat sieves said to be generally made by the Bushmen, but skin sieves by the Grass Bushmen and the Koranna-Hottentots. In English, after Díä!kwãin (L V.-25. VI note). Sieves supposed also to be used by the early race.

entries.title: Means of defending a dog from baboons
entries.snippet: A means of defending a dog from a Baboon, by telling him that it belongs to a young woman. (L V.-24. 5948-5956).

entries.title: Medicines taken by the ill
entries.snippet: Different Bushman Medicines; where found; and their uses. Only the names of these medicines are given in Bushman, and the remarks respecting them in English, after ǂkásin (L IV.-1. 3425-3440).

entries.title: Men enchanted by new maidens and changed into trees
entries.snippet: By a glance from the eye of a maiden (probably at a time when she would be usually kept in strict retirement) men became fixed in whatever position they then occupied, with whatever they were holding in their hands,…

entries.title: Men who hunted lions with bones
entries.snippet: Two brothers succesfully hunt Lions with bones, until the younger brother goes out hunting alone and is killed. The elder brother waits at home for his younger brother and the Lions come and kill him.

entries.title: Men who run away fear greatly (on courage and cowardice)
entries.snippet: A note by ||kabbo on courage and cowardice.

entries.title: Method of cooking and eating porcupine
entries.snippet: A description of the preparation, cooking, eating and distribution of porcupine meat among the people. The men eat from the backbone, because they want the most meat to be given to the children.

entries.title: Missing the game
entries.snippet: The women must not eat meat from the shoulder blades of the springbok, or the arrows will miss the game. This is called 'when we shoot our death'.

entries.title: Mixing arrow poisons
entries.snippet: How to combine |ßkururu poison with that of the puffadder's glands and Boom Gift to smear on arrow-heads to shoot game with.

entries.title: Mode of addressing rain
entries.snippet: Clouds. Preparations to be made for the coming rain; the springbok to be watched for, etc. (L VIII.-23. 8018-8029). The old men address the rain and the rain-clouds.

entries.title: Mode of eating porcupine
entries.snippet: Different customs concerning the eating of the Porcupine. By |hanǂkass'o (L VII.-6. 6590 rev.-6594 rev.).

entries.title: Moons' (months) possess their names
entries.snippet: The names for August and September were given by Díä!kwãin, by whom it was also stated that the names for all the months are known to the old people. In the Katkop dialect (L V.-10. 4796).

entries.title: More about sorcerers
entries.snippet: He changes himself into a jackal, and goes to find out what is detaining his people, when they do not return home. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-19. 5506 rev.-5512 rev.).

entries.title: More about the Day's Heart star: what he says to his daughter
entries.snippet: The Day's Heart star continues his discourse to his daughter, the Day's Heart child. He tells her about her mother (who resembles a Cat) and the doings, hunting and appearance of other animals.

entries.title: More about the hyena and its doings
entries.snippet: Habits of the Hyena (L II.-9. 921-927, 928-930). The hyena steals springbok from the lynx and the wild dog and takes it to its family in its hole. It does not hunt.

entries.title: More about the jackal and its doings
entries.snippet: How the Jackal feeds its children (L II.-9. 927 and 928). The jackal takes springbok flesh to his children.

entries.title: Moths coming to the fire of Bushmen by night foretell the killing of certain kinds of game
entries.snippet: The coming of different kinds of moths to the fire at night foretells the killing of certain types of game. The moths resemble different types of game.

entries.title: Mountain 'Bushmen'
entries.snippet: Mountain Bushman. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-22. 7982 rev.-7984 rev.). Regarding Mountain Bushmen. (L VIII.-26. 8280 rev.).

entries.title: Mr G. Stow's picture no. 3 (the ǁken dance)
entries.snippet: The Bushman Doctor or Sorcerer (who may be of either sex.) By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-20. 7757-7762, 7768-7774). Remarks upon sorcerers, their dress, etc.

entries.title: Names and descriptions of bags, nets and things worn
entries.snippet: Names and descriptions of nets, bags and things worn, found on an insert in the notebook.

entries.title: Names for certain winds
entries.snippet: Names for certain Winds. (L VIII.-13. 7196 rev.).

entries.title: Names for the star Canopus
entries.snippet: |xam names for the star Canopus (or |ßko a-ggu). Bushman doings and prayers when Canopus and his grandmother Sirius come out (L II.-37. 3348-3355).

entries.title: Names of !kweiten ta ǁken's relations
entries.snippet: A list of Dutch and |xam names of some of !kweiten ta ||ken's relations and their places. A good many genealogical notes and portions of Bushman family history have been taken down (B II.

entries.title: Names of friends, relations and fellow prisoners
entries.snippet: Names of |a!kunta's (Stoffel's) and ||kabbo's (Jantje's) relations and some details of personal history.

entries.title: Names of insects and notes on some of them (at the South African Museum)
entries.snippet: |hanǂkass'o identifies and describes various insects (and some animals) found in Bushmanland.

entries.title: Names of plants and animals and notes on their use
Names of stars: given by ǂkasin and Dia!kwain
entries.snippet: The names of a number of Stars have been ascertained with the kind help of Mr. Maclear. Besides a separate alphabetical index to the Stars, the names of some are given in a supplementary list (B XXV.

entries.title: Names of ǀhanǂkass'o's relations
entries.snippet: The names of some Bushmen. (L VIII.-13. 7195 rev.). Names of some of |hanǂkass'o's relations.

entries.title: Names of ǂkasin's father, mother and their children
entries.snippet: A good many genealogical notes and portions of Bushman family history have been taken down (B II. 349, 359 rev., 360-362, 364, 365 368-373, 376; BXXV. 2352, 2412 and 2416; L III.-1. 476-506; L IV.-1.

entries.title: Note on a certain man
entries.snippet: Names of various Bushmen and Bushman women, with information regarding them. Partly in Bushman by and partly in English after |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-1. 6078-6081, 4. 6370 rev., note, 11. 6969 rev., note 14.

entries.title: Note on kkuirri-ttu
entries.snippet: Personal information about a man called kkuirri-ttu (|hanǂkass'o's uncle) who was killed by a white man over some missing oxen.

entries.title: Note on lizards
entries.snippet: The ||horu resembles the kochelman, or !khau. Children eat them, but not grown-up people.

entries.title: Note on the two Lions
entries.snippet: A note about the two Lions, !gu and !haue ta ǂhou. !gu eats people, but !haue ta ǂhou does not and is usually the one who scolds !gu about his doings.

entries.title: Notes (to Children do not eat jackal's hearts)
entries.snippet: A description of some barter, containing allusion to the ill effect of eating Jackals' hearts (L VIII.-16. 7456 rev.-7458 rev., 7460, note).

entries.title: Notes on a bird (ǀkitten-ǀkitten)
entries.snippet: A bird found in Bushmanland called the |kitten-|kitten.

entries.title: Notes on chippings, or, Chipping no. 4
entries.snippet: Remarks concerning copy of Chipping, No. 4. (L VIII.-19. 7639-7642). The dots resemble those that the people paint onto other people's heads with tto (rooiklip, or haematite).

entries.title: Notes on rock painting copy no. 2 (!nu'sa and other groups)
entries.snippet: Remarks concerning copies of Bushman Paintings, given by Colonel Durnfold to His Excellency Sir Bartle Frere. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-22. 7969-7972, 7974 and 7975, 7983-7993, 23. 7995-8004).

entries.title: Notes on rock painting copy no. 3
entries.snippet: |hanǂkass'o describes a group of people who are fighting each other (as they appear in a copy of rock painting no. 3). A man on horseback has stabbed another.

entries.title: Notes on rock painting copy no. 5, no. 6. and no. 7
entries.snippet: Remarks concerning copies of Bushman Paintings, given by Colonel Durnfold to His Excellency Sir Bartle Frere. By /hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-22. 7969-7972, 7974 and 7975, 7983-7993, 23. 7995-8004).

entries.title: Notes on rock painting copy no. 8: the rain and the rain-bull
entries.snippet: Remarks concerning copies of Bushman Paintings, given by Colonel Durnfold to His Excellency Sir Bartle Frere.-By |hanǂkass'o. (L VIII.-22. 7969-7972, 7974 and 7975, 7983-7993, 23. 7995-8004).

entries.title: Notes on rock painting copy no. 9
entries.snippet: Remarks concerning copies of Bushman Paintings, given by Colonel Durnfold to His Excellency Sir Bartle Frere. By /hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-22. 7969-7972, 7974 and 7975, 7983-7993, 23. 7995-8004).

entries.title: Notes to the story of The Wind
entries.snippet: The Wind thought to be seen, in the form of a bird, by the brother-in-law of the narrator when a child. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-8. 6713-6724).

entries.title: Oh old woman! At what place did you grow up?
entries.snippet: An insert containing an address to an old woman in which she is asked where she grew up as a child and what the place's name is.

entries.title: On the 'Children of shortness' (the 'Grass Bushmen')
entries.snippet: The Grass Bushmen. (L V VIII.-22. 7968 rev. and 7969 re., 7976 and 7977, 7980 and 7981, 7980 rev. and 7081 rev.). A description of the Grass Bushmen and the names of places in their country.

entries.title: On women's hunting or ǀkua
entries.snippet: Women's hunting is called |kua. Women dig out things and 'hunt' prey such as 'Bushman rice'. They 'kill' the rice in its hole (here a figure of speech) because it runs about.

entries.title: Ostriches and barter
entries.snippet: About the hunting of ostriches, the finding and drinking of ostrich eggs, the preparation and cooking of ostrich meat and the bartering of ostrich feathers for tobacco with white men.

entries.title: Others living north of the Orange River
entries.snippet: Concerning certain Kafirs from the northern side of the Orange River. (L VIII.27. 8387-8390). The black people ('Kafirs') in |hanǂkass'o's country live to the north (or the 'other side') of the Orange River.

entries.title: Oud Bakkis or 'one nose'
entries.snippet: Names of various Bushmen and Bushman women, with information regarding them. Partly in Bushman by and partly in English after |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-1. 6078-6081, 4. 6370 rev., note, 11. 6969 rev., note 14.

entries.title: Oud Dorntje catches a leopard ('tiger')
entries.snippet: An account of how |kerri or Oud Dorntje and his brothers catch a leopard (translated as tiger), take it home, cook it and eat it.

entries.title: Parents instruct children how to get food, or, How D.H's father and mother instructed their children to get food
entries.snippet: Some Bushman children advised by their parents to be self-reliant in seeking food, in order to prepare themselves for the loss of their natural protectors. Related by Día!kwãin (L V.-6. 4404-4411 rev.-4413 rev.).

entries.title: Parts of the ostrich not to be eaten by children
entries.snippet: A certain portion of the Ostrich not to be eaten by children. In the Katkop dialect by Díä!kwãin (L V.-20. 5573 rev. and 5574 rev.).

entries.title: People fear the darkness's rain
entries.snippet: The people fear the rain that comes at night - it had come and flooded Victoria West, which was situated in a ravine.

entries.title: Personal history of Friedrich Hortnoop
entries.snippet: Name and other information regarding 'Friedrich Hortnoop', a relative of Díä!kwãin's (L VII.-1. 6046f, 60461, 6046k rev.).

entries.title: Personal history of Hendrick Beren and some words
entries.snippet: About Hendrik Beren, an employee of Mr Willmott, and some words given by him.

entries.title: Personal history of Jan Plat (he leaves Calvinia with Dia!kwain)
entries.snippet: An account of Jan Plat's movements with his travelling companion, Dia!kwain, and his meeting with his wife, who was keeping sheep in Louriet.

entries.title: Personal history of ǀhanǂkass'o
entries.snippet: Names of various Bushmen and Bushman women, with information regarding them. Partly in Bushman by and partly in English after /hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-1. 6078-6081, 4. 6370 rev., note, 11. 6969 rev., note 14.

entries.title: Personal history of ǁkabbo
entries.snippet: The names of ||kabbo's (Jantje Toorm's) mother (|koi ang) and some of his other relations. The piece includes details of his personal history and upbringing.

entries.title: Picture of Mr Orpen's
entries.snippet: A curious description of a dance and other doings of sorcerers was given in explanation of No. 3 of Mr Stow's copies of Bushman paintings (L V.-22. 5755-5775). Further details regarding sorcerers (suggested by Mr J.M.

entries.title: Places of the ǀxam
entries.snippet: The part of Bushmanland formerly occupied by ǁkábbo. Given by |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-14. 7215-7217). A place called '||gubo' by the |xam is called 'Blaauwpits' by the 'Bastaards' because the rocks there are black slate.

entries.title: Poison of the puffadder
entries.snippet: Snake Poison. By /hanǂkass'o (L VIII.23. 8040 and 8041). The effects of poison from the puffadder and another animal or snake.

entries.title: Porcupine hunting
entries.snippet: A description of porcupine hunting, in which the moonlight is of service, follows [The Mantis makes an eland] (L VIII.-6. 6506-6595 1/2). A story about the hunting of porcupine.

entries.title: Porcupine hunting
entries.snippet: An account of the hunting of porcupine and the practices that ensure its success (such as the treatment of their bones). The hunter is 'worked on' by the women before he goes to seek porcupine.

entries.title: Porcupine hunting: we go to sit waiting for a porcupine and some of the habits of the porcupine
entries.snippet: Hunting the Porcupine, its habits and faculties (L V.-7. 4436-4456). The hunters sleep outside the porcupine's house so that it cannot smell them.

entries.title: Porcupine's hole
entries.snippet: The porcupine's hole is stone on the outside and earth on the inside.

entries.title: Prayer to a star
entries.snippet: Prayer to the Stars. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-28. 8447-8449). Prayer of the narrator's grandfather to the star Canopus. (L VIII.-28. 8452-8458).

entries.title: Prayers to the Moon
entries.snippet: The |xam pray to the Moon, but not the Sun. They raise their hand and speak, asking for success with hunting springbok, finding ostrich eggs and 'digging out' 'Bushman rice' the next day.

entries.title: Quagga makes flour
entries.snippet: A description of the preparation of Meal, from the !kaùru ( . . . . . ) berries, was given, in the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin.

entries.title: Rain changes people into frogs
entries.snippet: The Rain believed to be ridden by sorcerers. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-27. 8399 rev. and 8400 rev., note).

entries.title: Rain protects frogs
entries.snippet: Frogs are not to be killed, lest drought ensue. An exhortation, to the Bushmen, to be careful of food in time of plenty, is followed by a description of the jackal's doings with regard to food, etc. (L VIII.-16.

entries.title: Rain washes out a dead man's footsteps
entries.snippet: Rain follows death. The method of protecting the grave. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-28. 8465-8467). When a person dies the rain falls, washing away their footprints ('a person who rains').

entries.title: Rain's animals
entries.snippet: The people fear the rain's animals because they are things the rain has put aside for its meat. They are the cobra, the puffadder, the tortoise and the great water tortoise.

entries.title: Rainbow (ǀkaggen and !kwammana)
entries.snippet: The Mantis and his son-in-law /kwámmana are both to be seen in the rainbow, the Mantis uppermost and /kwámmana underneath. (L VIII.-7. 6600 rev. & 6601 rev.).

entries.title: Rainmaking (a version by Dia!kwain)
entries.snippet: An account of rain-making by means of dragging a water-animal over the land, in illustration of a Bushman painting copied by Mr J.M.

entries.title: Rainmaking, when the wind is in the north
entries.snippet: Rain-making. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-1. 6093-6095). The work of the rainmakers; how they 'grasp' the rain's 'ribs'. Also about the rain and the north wind.

entries.title: Rainmaking: another story of it
entries.snippet: Rain-making: told to ǁkábbo by a Rain-maker who was his foster-father. (L II.-25. 2264-2319 1/2.) About the rainmaker, parts of the rain, the rain and seasons and clouds and the wind.

entries.title: Regarding houses
entries.snippet: Huts made by the Bushman women. The shelter for them made by the men. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-12. 7111 rev. and 7112 rev., note).

entries.title: Regarding the children of the Mantis
entries.snippet: The three children of the Mantis. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-12. 7074 rev.-7076 rev., note).

entries.title: Regarding the living again of male ostriches
entries.snippet: The male Ostrich believed to possess the power of returning once to life. (L VIII.31. 8750 rev.-8752 rev.). The male ostrich is the one who returns to life after it has died and is called ||khabbi-ssin.

entries.title: Relationships
entries.snippet: Bushman terms for various degrees of relationship. Given by |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-31. 8788-8790, 32. 8792 and 8793, 8845-8847). Terms for various relationships by marriage. (L VIII.-12. 7067 rev. and 7068 rev., 32.

entries.title: Relationships and other words (words and sentences)
entries.snippet: Bushman terms for various degrees of relationship. Given by |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-31. 8788-8790, 32. 8792 and 8793, 8845-8847). Terms for various relationships by marriage. (L VIII.-12. 7067 rev. and 7068 rev., 32.

entries.title: Remarks by ǀhanǂkass'o on the preceding story (The death of the !kau)
entries.snippet: |hanǂkass'o remarks on the story 'The death of the !kau' (kochelman, or Agama lizard): he says that the !khau had been on his way to the red sandhills because he wanted to live near to the shallow pools (called !kaugen…

entries.title: Remarks concerning copies of Bushman pictures nos. I-XXIV Collected By Mr H.C. Schunke, and deposited in the Grey Library
entries.snippet: Explanatory remarks concerning copies of Bushmen Paintings and Etchings, collected by Mr. H.C. Schunke and deposited in the Grey Library. Partly in Bushman by and partly in English after |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-1.

entries.title: Salutations to the sun and greetings to others
entries.snippet: Greetings and salutations to people and to the sun at various times of the day.

entries.title: Seeking springbok
entries.snippet: The |xam hunt springbok. Sentences and Words (B II. 350-353, 363; L II.-1. 196-201, 250 and 251; L II.-4. 476-478); further by /a!kúnta (B I. 67-157, etc.; L I.-1. 1-94, 97-114, 118; 2.

entries.title: Signs made on leaving a place
entries.snippet: Signs made by Bushmen in order to show the direction in which they have gone. By |hanǂkass'o.(L VIII.-27. 8374-8386).

entries.title: Sirius and Canopus
entries.snippet: |hanǂkass'o's step-grandmother (Ttuai-an) joyfully adressed Canopus and Sirius when there was enough food to eat.

entries.title: Sitting in the shade
entries.snippet: The shade not to be sat in, unless it is really summer. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-15. 5149 rev. and 5150 rev.).

entries.title: Skinning and cutting
entries.snippet: Stone-knives. (L VIII.-27. 8434). Reed and stone used for cutting purposes. (L VIII.-26. 8313 and 8314). The power of cutting possessed by a reed and by quartz. (L. VII.-21. 7826 rev. and 7827 rev., note).

entries.title: Sneezing
