entries.snippet: Sneezing believed to be a sign that the name of the person who sneezes has been uttered. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-18. 7594 rev. and 7595 rev., note).

entries.title: Sneezing: and searching for wives and families
entries.snippet: When he sneezes, ||kabbo believes his family is talking about him and about the |xam, their names, their masters, their families and their search for their wives.

entries.title: Sneezing: to be avoided when game is shot
entries.snippet: Sneezing to be avoided when game has been wounded. How to obviate the ill effect of a Sneeze. (L V.-21. 5654-5659). Mothers tell the children about how sneezing affects the death of game.

entries.title: Some of the stars (their names)
entries.snippet: Bushman names for Stars. Given by |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-32. 8826 rev., 8842 and 8843, 8841 rev. and 8842 rev.). The stars (in Orion's belt) are named according to the hunting of springbok.

entries.title: Song of the !kau's child
entries.snippet: The song of the !khau lizard's child, which it sings while following its father home after he has cut off his own flesh.

entries.title: Sorcerers are like lions
entries.snippet: The sorcerers see a handsome person and cause him to be ill. The dogs cannot sleep for barking at the sorcerers, and the sick man dies, although he has been doctored. (L VIII.-15. 7298-7303).

entries.title: Sorcerers shoot with invisible arrows, causing illness
entries.snippet: Sorcerers shoot with invisible arrows, causing illness. The sick man. The aid of the old women to be sought. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-14. 7287 and 7288; 15. 7289-7295). The wounded person feels pain and weariness.

entries.title: Spoken of a parrot in the village of Mowbray
entries.snippet: How a parrot can make the lateral click like a person. (L VIII.29. 8556 rev., note). A parrot seen by |hanǂkass'o in Mowbray sounds like a person, because it can make the lateral click.

entries.title: Springbok bones
entries.snippet: A description of the springboks' bones and the |xam names given to them.

entries.title: Springbok ewes and lamb's cries
entries.snippet: The mother springbok makes sounds to her kid as she travels and the young springbok responds: the mothers grunt and the kids bleat.

entries.title: Springbok horns
entries.snippet: A young ram springbok grows horns on his forehead when he becomes a grown-up springbok.

entries.title: Springbok hunting
entries.snippet: When it seems as if the rain will break, the people start seeking springbok. An account of the driving and shooting of springbok, and hunting methods in general.

entries.title: Springbok possess magic arrows
entries.snippet: The springbok possess magic or invisible arrows which make people ill. Little children are not allowed to play on the springbok's skin, because the springbok gets under the people's flesh and makes them…

entries.title: Ssa ka Kumm (the eland's story)
entries.snippet: The hunting of eland and related ceremonies. There are many things the hunter must do to show respect for the eland, as it is a 'magic thing' and |kaggen (the Mantis) will try to revive the game and keep it from dying.

entries.title: Ssauken (a game)
entries.snippet: Ssauken. A game played among the Bushmen, in which both sexes appear to take part. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-26. 8335-8350). It seems to resemble a dance in the people's movements.

entries.title: Ssho ǀoa or ǁkarruken-ǁkarruken or ǀu ssho a
entries.snippet: Shó-|õä, a Bushman vegetable medicine, used also as a charm (L II.-36. 3242-3261).

entries.title: Stars and death
entries.snippet: Death. The stars know the time at which a Bushman dies, and the fall of one announces, to those who are not aware of it, that something bad has happened.

entries.title: Stars and flowers
entries.snippet: The Stars were formerly people. Some details regarding their singing. The opening of flowers from their buds compared with the former ways of the star. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L.V.-21.

entries.title: Stars and game
entries.snippet: Stars and game behave in a similar way: when an animal is wounded the stars arouse its heart and make it want to walk.

entries.title: Stoffel's grandmother
entries.snippet: The narrator's grandmother restored to life. Related by /a!kúnta. (L I.-2. 133) |a!kunta's (Stoffel's) grandmother (his 'oud mamma') is dead. The people come back to look (to 'smell') for her but she has gone away.

entries.title: Stones which kill the thrower
entries.snippet: An account, apparently a legend, called in my first report 'Stones which kill the thrower', given by /a!kúnta in very early days (L I.-2. 125 and 126), wants revision and further explanation to render it intelligible.

entries.title: Story of the Lion and the Jackals
entries.snippet: A fable, somewhat similar to the Hottentot one of the lion and the jackal who went to shoot elands ('Reynard the Fox in South Africa', p.3, etc.), was told in the Katkop dialect, by ǂkásin (L IV.-1.

entries.title: Story of the Lion and the Jackals: another translation which is a little different
entries.snippet: A fable, somewhat similar to the Hottentot one of the lion and the jackal who went to shoot elands ('Reynard the Fox in South Africa', p.3, etc.), was told in the Katkop dialect, by ǂkásin (L IV.-1.

entries.title: Story of the Moon and the Hare and how the Moon punished the Hare
entries.snippet: The veritable Hottentot myth of the 'Origin of Death' is told in the Katkop dialect by ǂkásin, whose father was a Hottentot; and, although the narrator says that he heard the story from his mother /'wá ǂkõ, a Bushwoman,…

entries.title: Story of the Moon and the Hare: a version at first by ǂkasin and then by Dia!kwain
entries.snippet: ...the Moon's mother, who, according to the first version (begun by ǂkásin, but mainly related by Dia!kwãin), died in consequence of the wrong message delivered to men by the Hare; whereupon the angered Moon split the…

entries.title: Story of the Moon and the Hare: a version by Dia!kwain
entries.snippet: ...the Hare announces to the Moon the death of the Moon's mother, thereby causing his wrath, etc. (L IV.-4.

entries.title: Story of the Moon and the Hare: a version by ǂkasin
entries.snippet: The veritable Hottentot myth of the "Origin of Death" is told in the Katkop dialect by ǂkásin, whose father was a Hottentot; and, although the narrator says that he heard the story from his mother, a Bushwoman, its…

entries.title: Story of ǀkua ka khumm
entries.snippet: ... a cunning woman of this [Early] race, who lived alone in a house underground, deceived and robbed /kwámmana [in this story he is called |kua ka khumm].

entries.title: Story of ǂkasin's hunting adventures
entries.snippet: The narrator's own hunting adventures. He kills a hyena and her two young ones, and shoots some springbok which had been startled by a lion. The presence of jackals indicates the lion's whereabouts.

entries.title: Sun and Moon story
entries.snippet: A continuation of the The Sun pierces the full Moon with his knife, in which the Moon asks the Sun to leave his /kuirri (piece of neck sinew) so that the children can still see him come.

entries.title: Sun, Moon and stars
entries.snippet: Various statements are given with regard to the nature and movements of the celestial bodies. The first of these treats of the Moon and Stars, ending with the 'Bushman-rice' Star Canopus (B I.

entries.title: Swallows
entries.snippet: Stones must not be thrown at the !kuerri-|nan (a little bird which is supposed to belong to the Rain). The evil effects of so doing exemplified in the case of the narrator's cousin.

entries.title: Tactics in springbok hunting
entries.snippet: |hanǂkass'o describes the tactics used in hunting springbok (referring to a numbered picture), in which ostrich feathers attached to sticks are stuck into the small bushes to resemble a person and divert the…

entries.title: Terms of relationship
entries.snippet: Bushman terms for various degrees of relationship. Given by |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-31. 8788-8790, 32. 8792 and 8793, 8845-8847). Terms for various relationships by marriage. (L VIII.-12. 7067 rev. and 7068 rev., 32.

entries.title: The !gixa (sorcerer) ǀkaunu
entries.snippet: The Rain-maker and Sorcerer, /kaùnu. (L VIII.-31. 8759-8762, 8743 rev., 8748 rev.).

entries.title: The !gwiten who was niggardly to his wife
entries.snippet: The Wild Dog (or !gwiten) is stingy ('niggardly') to his wife (when they were people of the Early Race). He gives his wife the lean male pig, but takes the fat sow for himself, and she becomes thinner and thinner.

entries.title: The !ho
entries.snippet: In a peculiar state of the atmosphere, in which sounds can be heard at a considerable distance, sitting, instead of standing, is recommended; for fear of evil consequences.

entries.title: The !ho and Ko-boken
entries.snippet: Dia!kwain's brother (Ko-boken) is standing up at dawn, despite warnings against doing so from his father. A little whirlwind, or !ho, comes and pushes him over and he staggers and has to lie down.

entries.title: The !k'anni is an ornament worn by men and women
entries.snippet: The !k'anni is an ornament worn by men and women. |hanǂkass'o says they are beautiful and look like things from the sea.

entries.title: The !ka-ka ǀkhueten (a spider)
entries.snippet: A note about a certain spider called the !ka-ka |khueten.

entries.title: The !kabbi: a bird which has white legs and is eaten
entries.snippet: About the !kabbi, or partridge, named by |han-kass'o after seeing one in a picture.

entries.title: The !kain who snatched off the hair of the Ostrich's head, and put it on his own head
entries.snippet: The !käin (formerly a man of the early race, and now a little bird which is said to resemble the Lanius collaris), the Ostrich, and the Lizard. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-30. 8668-8670).

entries.title: The !kau who brought home his own flesh as food
entries.snippet: A tale of the Early Race about the !khau lizard, who brings home his own flesh as food so that his wife will think he has brought home a Quagga.

entries.title: The !kh'o: a blue mist which resembles smoke
entries.snippet: Brief mention is made of a kind of blue mist, which resembles fire-smoke, and is illness. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-20. 5557-5561).

entries.title: The !khau carried off by a Lion
entries.snippet: The !kháü (a Lizard of the Genus Agama) who would not listen to the advice of his wife, and was carried off by a lion. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-18. 7626-7638, 20. 7728-7745).

entries.title: The !khau lizard and the rainclouds - springbok hunting follows rain; and the song of the !khau lizard
entries.snippet: The !khaü (a lizard of the genus Agama), which interferes with the coming of the rain-clouds. Springbok hunting follows the rain. (L VIII.-3. 6232 rev.-6234 rev., 6259-6266, 6269-6271).

entries.title: The !khou or water tortoise
entries.snippet: About the !khou, or water tortoise: it lives in water and is a rain's 'thing' (!khoa ka ||kerri-ssi !kau).

entries.title: The !koa (or Muishond)
entries.snippet: A song of the Early Race in which the !koa (or Muishond) cuts off his own flesh with his knife and cooks and eats it, until his body grows small from all the cutting. He asks the west wind what to do.

entries.title: The !kuerre-!kuerre (a bird)
entries.snippet: About the !kuerre-!kuerre bird and what people say when it flutters its wings.

entries.title: The !kuerri ǀnan and the rain
entries.snippet: [not translated]

entries.title: The !kwai !kwai, the Mantis and the children
entries.snippet: The !kwái-!kwái, the Mantis, and the Children. The !kwái-!kwái, who was formerly a man, and is now a bird (in appearance somewhat resembling a 'duiker'), comes, during the absence of their parents, to kill the children.

entries.title: The !kwana thorn tree
entries.snippet: Names of Trees and Plants, and other information regarding them, given partly in Bushman by and partly in English after |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-14. 7219 rev. and 7220 rev., note, 18. 7552 rev.-7754 rev., 21.

entries.title: The !nabbe
entries.snippet: The mode of preparation of the !nábbe ( . . . . ), made from the tail hair of various animals. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-11. 7005 rev.-7007 rev.).

entries.title: The 'Bushman doctor'
entries.snippet: The Bushman Doctor or Sorcerer (who may be of either sex). By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-20. 7757-7762, 7768-7774).

entries.title: The adhesive substance (ǀkwae) used in arrowmaking and its preparation for use
entries.snippet: Arrow making, and Arrow poisoning. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-26. 8293-8302, 8315-8334). Two kinds of Arrow. (L VIII.-10. 6923 rev. and 6924 rev., note). Arrow heads, etc. (L VIII.-1. 6086 and 6087).

entries.title: The adventure of a girl, named Ttai-tchuen, with a lioness who had young cubs
entries.snippet: Dia!kwain's cousin, Ttai tchuen, escapes from a lioness by remembering all the things her parents taught her about the doings of lions.

entries.title: The adventure of ǀkhui- ǀa with a family of baboons
entries.snippet: |khui- |a is out hunting and shoots a mother baboon who is carrying a child. The father baboon goes to find the child and asks |khui- |a to leave it alone and not kill it, as he has already taken the baby's mother from…

entries.title: The Anteater, Springbok and Lynx (ǁkabbo's version 1)
entries.snippet: ... the daughter of the springbok, here named !kháuko, is stolen away by the lynx, who married her.

entries.title: The Anteater, Springbok and Lynx (ǁkabbo's version 2)
entries.snippet: The Anteater inquires from a flock of springbok ewes, one after another, whether her child is a female.

entries.title: The Anteater, the young Springbok, the Lynx and the Partridge
entries.snippet: The Anteater, the young Springbok, the Lynx, and the Partridge, by lhanǂkass'o (L VIII.-29. 8561-8602). An Anteater (a person of the Early Race) steals a female Springbok child and disappears into her hole.

entries.title: The Anteater's laws (ǀa!kunta's version)
entries.snippet: Accounting for the habits of animals, in which various animals marry their own kind, eat certain things and behave in certain ways, because the Anteater tells them to.

entries.title: The Anteater's laws (ǁkabbo's version 2)
entries.snippet: An account of what the Anteater says various animals should eat, who they should marry, where they live, and what they look and smell like and why. This story is from the Early Times when people were animals.

entries.title: The Anteater's story, or, The Anteater, Springbok, Lynx and Partridge
entries.snippet: A more concise version of the fable of the anteater, springbok, lynx, and partridge, in the Katkop dialect, was given by !kwéiten ta ǁkën (L VI.-1. 3916-3929).

entries.title: The approach of strangers makes Bushmen sleepy
entries.snippet: The approach of strangers causes us to become drowsy. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-4. 6379 rev. and 6380 rev.).

entries.title: The avoidance of the name of the lion
entries.snippet: The name of the Lion not to be spoken by children. In the Katkop dialect, by Día!kwãin, from ǂkámme-an (L V.-7. 4526 and 4526, 8. 4527-4562). Another version of this warning (L V.-8. 4563-4573).

entries.title: The Brachycerus used as a means of cure for little children; the same insect not used again when another child is ill
entries.snippet: A ceremony performed with the Brachycerus (African Ground Weevil) in order to preserve young babies from convulsions. In the Katkop dialect by Díä!kwãin (L V.-23.

entries.title: The broken string
entries.snippet: The sorcerer !nuin|kúïten. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-15. 5079-5101, 5104-5109). A song sung by Xaa-ttin (Dia!kwain's father) after the death of the magician !nuin-|kui-ten.

entries.title: The Bushman doctress and sorceress Ttanno !khauken
entries.snippet: An old sorceress (a relative who befriended the narrator's family), her power of turning herself into a lioness, her pet springbok, etc. By Dia!kwãin (L V.-10. 4707-4743).

entries.title: The Bushmen's presentiments of things that are going to happen
entries.snippet: Bushman Presentiments. They feel in their bodies that certain events are going to happen. There is a kind of beating of the flesh, which tells them things.

entries.title: The calling of Ttuai-an's name
entries.snippet: Explanation of various personal names was given, in the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-10. 4751-4754, 4758-4777, 18. 5373 rev., 19. 5505 rev. and 5506 rev., 20. 5572 and 5573, 5605-5607).

entries.title: The Cat's song
entries.snippet: The Cat sings of being abused by the Lynx because he doesn't run fast and is not cunning like the Lynx, but he does deceive his pursuers. The Cat's song, before she was attacked by the Mantis (L II.-35.

entries.title: The children are sent to throw the sleeping Sun into the sky
entries.snippet: The Sun, a man from whose armpit brightness proceeded, lived formerly on earth; but only gave light for a space around his house.

entries.title: The children of the First Bushmen (who preceded the Flat Bushmen in their country) throw up the sleeping Sun into the sky, or, The children of the !khwe ǀna ssho !ke, ordered by their mothers, throw the sleeping Sun into the sky (a second version of the story)
entries.snippet: The Sun, a man from whose armpit brightness proceeded, lived formerly on earth; but only gave light for a space around his house.

entries.title: The children taught to use another name for the lion
entries.snippet: The name of the Lion not to be spoken by children. In the Katkop dialect, by Día!kwãin, from ǂkámme-an (L V.-7. 4526 and 4526, 8. 4527-4562). Another version of this warning (L V.-8. 4563-4573).

entries.title: The collecting of ostrich eggs
entries.snippet: A man collects ostrich eggs in a sack to take home to his wife. She breaks them open with a stone, then places sticks into the holes she has made, stirs up the inside of each egg and pours them out into a cooking pot…

entries.title: The coming of lion
entries.snippet: When the lion is coming, he is preceded by an apparition which resembles a real lion. By |hanǂkass'o (L. VIII.-6. 6576 rev.).

entries.title: The coming of the Mantis [the mantis] to sit upon the quiver of the Bushman father at home foretells the shooting of a hartebeest
entries.snippet: The |kaggen (or the mantis) that sits on the hunter's quiver foretells that the owner has successfully shot and killed a hartebeest on the hunting-ground.

entries.title: The consequences of a woman's smelling fresh Ssho ǀoa scent
entries.snippet: If women smell the Ssho |oa's scent (of a plant that is really new or fresh) they become very ill. The newly-brought Shó-|õä is feared by the women (L II.-36. 3279-3286).

entries.title: The Crow's story: the Crows are sent out to search for husbands, or, The !kagen ka Kkomm's story and the ǀhunn ta kkomm's story (including What happened when the !kagen found the missing men, p.3995)
entries.snippet: Bushman women send out crows to ascertain what has become of their husbands, who have not returned from hunting; they hang fat round the necks of the crows as food for their journey.

entries.title: The crying of the wind is an evil omen
entries.snippet: The crying of the Wind believed to forebode evil. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-23. 5841-5845).

entries.title: The crying of the wind tells beasts of prey where to find people
entries.snippet: The crying of the Wind tells the beasts of prey where to find people, and when it blows strongly they can approach the dwelling unheard, etc. (L V.-23. 5846-5857).

entries.title: The cutting and piercing of parts of the body
entries.snippet: Cutting off the top of the little finger, and piercing ears and nose. (L II.-2. 357-361.) Describing the cutting of the fingertips, hands and arms of male and female children, and the piercing of the nose and ears of…

entries.title: The Day's Heart star
entries.snippet: The Day's Heart star buries his child alive and goes away. Other creatures come by including the child's mother.

entries.title: The Day's Heart star child
entries.snippet: A small, but interesting piece, including the address of the younger sister to the wife of the Dawn's-Heart, when she had been transformed into a lioness, was given by /a !kúnta, who had heard it from a sister. (L I.-2.

entries.title: The death of the !kau (a lizard of the genus Agama)
entries.snippet: A story of the !khau (the Kochelman, or Agama lizard) in the Early Times when animals were people (7206opp). As he passes through the mountains, they squeeze and break him.

entries.title: The death of ǀkannu the Rain's man who was ǁkabbo's person
entries.snippet: A note by ||kabbo on |kannu, a 'Rain's man', who was ||kabbo's !kui or person after his father's death. |kannu was shot in the dark by his grandson (called !kauken ka |a).

entries.title: The death of ǁkabbo's brother and sister-in-law
entries.snippet: ||kabbo's brother was murdered because he stayed away from his wife and children and did not feed them. ||kabbo's sister-in-law later died of an 'internal sickness'.

entries.title: The digging out of 'Bushman rice'
entries.snippet: Bushman rice' may be dug by both men and women. (L VIII.-10. 6888 rev., note). Others look for gambro and catch korhaan and hares when there are no springbok to hunt.

entries.title: The doings of a family of lions
entries.snippet: An account of the doings of a family of lions (apparently a continuation of one of the Ichneumon's speeches, in the myth of the Mantis) gives a good deal of conversation of such a description as might naturally be…

entries.title: The doings of the jackals
entries.snippet: The Jackal and the Hyena' (in which piece there are interminable speeches by the Jackal) contains many allusions to other fables, and really forms part of the lengthy discourse of the Dawn's-Heart (L II.-19.

entries.title: The dream which Dia!kwain had before he received the tidings of the death of his father
entries.snippet: The dream of Díä!kwãin, before he received news of his father's death. Further omens, etc. In the Katkop dialect by Díä!kwãin (L V.-15. 5110-5146).

entries.title: The drought which caused ǀhanǂkass'o's grandparents to starve
entries.snippet: |hanǂkass'o's maternal grandmother, ǂkammi, was lean with starvation during the drought in their country, and his grandfather almost died from starvation during the same drought.

entries.title: The east wind
entries.snippet: Wind and Weather. The Four Winds. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-1. 6096-6101). The east wind is cold and blows strongly. The people have to make shelters and light fires to warm themselves when it comes.

entries.title: The eating of baboons
entries.snippet: Baboons are eaten by some groups of |xam and other peoples (such as the 'Berg Bushmen', the 'Groot Rivier's Bushmen' and the Koranna).

entries.title: The effect of a new maiden's gaze
entries.snippet: If a pubertal girl sees the men bringing game home they become fixed to the ground.

entries.title: The escape of ǀkannan from the Koranna commando
entries.snippet: /kannan, who warned the people in vain of the approach of a Koranna war party, and was the only one who escaped. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-26. 8269-8285).

entries.title: The explanation of the name Ssu-!kui-ten-tta (or Snore-White-Lying)
entries.snippet: [A] note explaining the curious name of the son of a sorceress.(L V.-9. 4652 and 4651 rev.). The naming of the Bushman Ssu-!kui-ten-tta (or Snore-White-Lying or 'Witbooi').

entries.title: The father-in-law and the mother-in-law
entries.snippet: The Wife's Parents. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-32. 8843-8845). The way in which sons-in-law speak to their parents-in-law.

entries.title: The gemsbok is a wind's thing
entries.snippet: The gemsbok watches and listens and smells the wind. It runs and is a wild 'wind's thing'.

entries.title: The girl of the Early Race who killed the children of the Rain
entries.snippet: The Girl (of the Early Race) who killed the Children of the Rain, bringing, thereby, severe punishment upon herself and those who lived with her. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-17. 7473-7519).

entries.title: The girl who made locusts
entries.snippet: A girl takes |kui sse hair and throws it into the sky, where it becomes locusts, which jump away.

entries.title: The girl who made the Milky Way, by throwing ashes into the sky
entries.snippet: A girl of the ancient race (preceding the Bushmen) wished for a little light, so that the people might see to return home by night. She, therefore, threw wood-ashes into the sky, which became the Milky Way.

entries.title: The girl who snaps her fingers at her parents and the rain
entries.snippet: The rain is angry with us, if we talk to a maiden against her wish. (L V.20. 5608-5612).

entries.title: The girl, of the Early Race of people, who married a Baboon
entries.snippet: A girl is becoming a new maiden and goes to lie down (in her hut). She says the Baboon's name. The Baboon sings to her so that her face will change shape and resemble his own.

entries.title: The giving of nicknames
entries.snippet: People give each other nicknames. For example Soppie (or Hendrick) was so called (by Andries, or !kaogen-ǂkakken) because he poured soup down himself.

entries.title: The great Star !gaunu, which singing named the stars
entries.snippet: The great star !gãunu, which singing, named the stars. By the position of certain stars, named by !gãunu, the porcupine knows the time for returning home. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-20. 5576-5580).

entries.title: The hail is the rain's legs
entries.snippet: The hail considered to be the legs of the rain. (L VIII.-7. 6652 rev., note). Also, some words pertaining to rain and related weather conditions.

entries.title: The hartebeest and the eland belong to the Mantis
entries.snippet: An account of the magic protection afforded by the Mantis to the Hartebeest and to the Eland (that to the latter being chiefly described here), dictated, in the Katkop dialect, by Día!kwãin (L V.-6. 4411-4434).

entries.title: The hartebeest resembles the Mantis
entries.snippet: The hartebeest's head resembles that of |kaggen (the Mantis), so it feels itself to be 'Mantis's thing'.

entries.title: The hunting, preparation and eating of ostrich, 'chiansbok', springbok, khoran, hare and jackal
entries.snippet: Narratives which occur in several episodes describing ||kabbo's hunting of various animals for his family, as well as the preparation of food.

entries.title: The hyena
entries.snippet: The hyena ('wolf') sleeps covered in earth. It leaps from the ground and runs away when people come, because it fears them. They chase and shoot it and burn off its hair.

entries.title: The Hyena and the Lion (1st version)
entries.snippet: Not only in the astrological mythology, but also in the world of Bushman Fable, the lion naturally occupies a prominent position.

entries.title: The hyena carries ostrich meat home to his children and the jackal which picks the backbone
entries.snippet: Habits of the Hyena (L II.-9. 921-927, 928-930). The hyena carries meat from the body of the ostrich to its family in its hole and picks flesh from the ostrich's backbone.

entries.title: The Hyena's revenge
entries.snippet: The Lion, the Jackal, the Chersina angulata, and the Hyena. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-25. 8232-8251). The Lion and the Jackal throw mud at each other and as a result the Lion sits blinded at the water.

entries.title: The Ichneumon rebukes the Mantis for his ill deeds
entries.snippet: The Ichneumon rebukes |kaggen (the Mantis) for fighting every time he meets someone, and for being foolish - his wife waits hungry for him at home while he is out fighting.

entries.title: The Ichneumon's discourse to the Mantis
entries.snippet: The account of the Mantis when he takes away the eggs of a certain fabulous bird, named !káken!káka káuï, is very curious.

entries.title: The intelligence and timidity of the jackal
entries.snippet: The people laugh at the jackal because it is intelligent and timid.

entries.title: The Jackal's speech, or, the Jackals and a springbok which the Hyena takes away from them
entries.snippet: The Jackal and the Hyena' (in which piece there are interminable speeches by the Jackal) contains many allusions to other fables, and really forms part of the lengthy discourse of the Dawn's-Heart, (L II.-19.

entries.title: The Korannas brought guns (while they felt that they had not a little cattle)
entries.snippet: |hanǂkass'o speaks of the guns brought by the Koranna to his people's place when they acquired cattle. Before then the Koranna had used blades.

entries.title: The Korhaan marries his elder sister (the Anteater's laws) (ǁkabbo's version 2)
entries.snippet: The Korhaan Malkop marries his elder sister, who carries him like a child to deceive others into thinking he is a child, even though he is her husband.

entries.title: The kwa kwara, or korhaan malkop
entries.snippet: What the korhaan malkop or the kwa kwara (also known as the bustard, the black koran or Eupodotis afra) sings when people startle it and what it sings when it stands still on the ground.

entries.title: The Lion and the man's story; what the Lion formerly did to the man or The young man of the Ancient Race, who was carried off by a Lion, when asleep in the field
entries.snippet: The young man of the ancient race, who was carried off by a Lion, when asleep in the field. A very affecting legend, in the Katkop dialect, related to Dia!kwãin by his mother ǂkámme-an (L V.-7. 4457-4525).

entries.title: The Lion and the Muishond (continued from The two Lions: pointers to the Southern Cross)
entries.snippet: A Muishond is in its hole and runs away, but stops because its side is decaying. The Lion follows its scent and takes the decayed piece in its mouth.

entries.title: The Lion and the Tortoise (continued from The two Lions: pointers to the Southern Cross)
entries.snippet: The two Lions come upon a Tortoise. The Lion called !gu swallows the Tortoise down whole while it is still alive.

entries.title: The lion has the power of turning itself into other things
entries.snippet: The Lion is believed to possess the power of assuming other forms. (L VIII.-23. 8075-8077). It assumes the form of a hartebeest because it wants the people to try and head it as if it were game.

entries.title: The Lion star
entries.snippet: The (two) Lions, which was the name given to us (by our first informants) for the Pointers to the Southern Cross, were formerly men, and at the same time lions. (LII.-1.

entries.title: The Lion star and other stars
entries.snippet: The (two) Lions, which was the name given to us (by our first informants) for the Pointers to the Southern Cross, were formerly men, and at the same time lions (LII.-1. 277 and 278).

entries.title: The lion's dream
entries.snippet: A full-grown Bushman, whom the narrator knew, punished by a lion for having eaten forbidden portions of the lion's prey, as a child. Told by Día!kwãin.(L V.-8. 4574-4617 1/2, 9. 4618, with notes upon 4616 rev.

entries.title: The Lion's story
entries.snippet: ... the account of a man who, wishing to escape from a lion, cut off his sore leg, and ran successfully away upon the sound one. It was told by !kwéiten ta ||kên (L VI.-1. 3959-3969).

entries.title: The little porcupine
entries.snippet: The people wait for the porcupines to return to their hole and kill them as they return, including the female porcupine who returns last.

entries.title: The Lizard, the Mice and the Mantis (includes the song of the Agama lizard)
entries.snippet: The !kháü (a lizard of the genus Agama) and his daughter, the Mice, and the Mantis. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.30. 8671-8702, 31. 8703-8736).

entries.title: The locust bird or ǁkerri
entries.snippet: The locusts (with the locust birds which accompany them) are set free by Sorcerers. Stones not to be thrown at the locusts. (L V.-21. 5708-5719 1/2).

entries.title: The loss of ǁkabbo's tobacco bag, which was stolen by a hungry dog, belonging to !gou !nui, named 'Blom'
entries.snippet: An account of how ||kabbo's tobacco pouch was stolen by a dog ('Blom') while he slept at night, and of the subsequent search for it.

entries.title: The lynx; its flesh eaten by Bushmen, but not by the Bushman women; the manner of hunting it, etc.
entries.snippet: The flesh of the Lynx not eaten by Bushman women. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-12. 7103-7113).

entries.title: The maiden who snaps her fingers at the rain (and causes lightning)
entries.snippet: We should not allow a maiden to snap her fingers at us; the wrath of the Rain against us being thereby aroused. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-20. 5618-5622, 5624).

entries.title: The maiden who was taken up in a whirlwind by the agency of the angry Rain and became a great snake
entries.snippet: The Maiden who would not listen to her parents, and was punished, together with those around her, by the angry Rain. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-13. 4981-5022).

entries.title: The maiden's story; the frog's story
entries.snippet: A note has been made, from ǁkábbo's information, of a story not yet written down in Bushman, telling how the Rain carried off a girl belonging to the First Bushmen (or the ancient people living before the Bushmen), who…

entries.title: The making of a needle from a springbok's foreleg
entries.snippet: Flat Bushmen' make a needle for sewing from the foreleg bone of a springbok; what the bone (|kao) and the needle (ǂoro) are called. The bones of all things are each named.

entries.title: The making of clay pots
entries.snippet: The making of Clay Pots. By /hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-23. 8054-8067). The women collect earth and male grass. They mould the wet earth and work it into a pot, and when it is just dry they rub fat on the pot.

entries.title: The man who ordered his wife to cut off his ears
entries.snippet: The man (belonging to the early race) who ordered his wife to cut off his ears. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-12. 7095-7097).

entries.title: The man who sought refuge from the rain in a cave, and found a lion there before him
entries.snippet: The lion made the rain in order to confuse the man so he wouldn't know where his house was and the lion could catch him. The lion made it rain and waited in a cave for the man who sought refuge there.

entries.title: The man who went to sleep when out hunting alone
entries.snippet: Another legend relates how a man of this early race was killed and eaten by a lion; and it is used to point out the folly of venturing to fall asleep when out alone in the field. Told also by !kwéiten ta ǁkën (L VI.-2.

entries.title: The manner of dividing fat
entries.snippet: Fat; division of into small quantities.(L VIII-12. 7108 rev.-7110 rev., note).

entries.title: The Mantis - giver of names to places
entries.snippet: The Mantis is further said, by the Bushmen, to have given places their names. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-12. 7033 rev. and 7034 rev., note).

entries.title: The Mantis and !goe !kweitentu
entries.snippet: Among the fights which the Mantis has with different personages and animals, one of the most interesting to the Bushman mind, is that with a being whose eyes are in his feet, instead of his head, which is smooth.

entries.title: The Mantis and !kaken-!kaka-!k'aui (another version)
entries.snippet: The account of the Mantis when he takes away the eggs of a certain fabulous bird, named !káken!káka láuï, is very curious.

entries.title: The Mantis and Koro-tuiten
entries.snippet: The Mantis and Kóro-tuíten. The Mantis learns from Kóro-tuíten how to obtain 'Bushman-rice' (i.e. larvae of the ants) with ease; but, acting ungratefully towards his instructor, he soon loses his newly acquired power.

entries.title: The Mantis and the Cat
entries.snippet: The Cat walks along singing of the Lynx who has derided her for not being able to run. |kaggen (the Mantis) and the Cat fight but the Cat puts its head in the ground and |kaggen beats its tail instead.

entries.title: The Mantis and the Great Tortoise
entries.snippet: |kaggen (the Mantis) strokes the neck of the Great Tortoise and she pulls her head into her neck hole, so his arms get stuck and decay.

entries.title: The Mantis and the hunting of eland
entries.snippet: What the |kaggen (Mantis) does to see whether the people respect the eland.

entries.title: The Mantis and the Ticks
entries.snippet: The Mantis visits the abode of the Ticks, attempts to take some of their food, and is well beaten by them. He flies away, goes into the water, and returns home where he is pitied and lectured by the Ichneumon.

entries.title: The Mantis and the Ticks
entries.snippet: The Mantis visits the abode of the Ticks, attempts to take some of their food, and is well beaten by them. He flies away, goes into the water, and returns home where he is pitied and lectured by the Ichneumon.
entries.date_label: 30 - 31 October 1878 (translated in October 1919 by D Bleek)

entries.title: The Mantis and ǀkwammana visit the Dassie's house
entries.snippet: The Mantis and |kwámmana go out visiting together. The Mantis involves himself in a quarrel with a young "Dasse" (Hyrax), and stones fall upon him and |kwámmana.

entries.title: The Mantis and ǁkhwai-hemm
entries.snippet: ...the visit of ǁkhwái-hemm (the All-devourer) to the Mantis. The latter, by means of the Porcupine, invites ǁkhwái-hemm to visit him. He comes, and presently swallows /kwámmana and the Mantis.

entries.title: The Mantis makes an eland
entries.snippet: The Mantis (/kaggen) makes and brings up a young eland. /kwámmana, who is informed of its existence by the Ichneumon, kills it, to the grief of /kaggen.

entries.title: The Mantis pretends he has left one of his veldschoens behind and becomes a Lion
entries.snippet: The Ichneumon ... proceeds to comment upon some other doings of the Mantis, who - wanting to roast the little 'Löffelhund' (Proteles) - is himself roasted by its incensed mother; and, escaping, dips his singed wings…

entries.title: The Mantis takes away the Tick's sheep (including Porcupine's speech concerning the coming of ǁkhwai-hemm)
entries.snippet: The visit of the Mantis to the houses of the Ticks, who, excepting one boy, have hidden themselves away in the fleeces of their sheep.

entries.title: The Mantis, his wife and their things
entries.snippet: ... an explanation that the Moon has the power of talking, because he belongs to the Mantis, all of whose things talk (L II.-6. 670-677).

entries.title: The Mantis, the Ichneumon and ǀkammanga go to Lion's house
entries.snippet: The Ichneumon, |kaggen (the Mantis) and |kwammana go to the Lion's house. |kaggen is afraid and hides in a bag. He frightens a little Lion who looks into the bag and the mother Lion grows angry with |kaggen.

entries.title: The Mantis, the Mice and the Beetle
entries.snippet: The Mantis, the valiant Striped Mouse [Mus Pumilio, Common Striped Field-Mouse of the Cape], the other Mice, and the Beetle . By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-17. 7542-7549, 18. 7550).

entries.title: The marking of arrows
entries.snippet: Arrow making, and Arrow poisoning. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-26. 8293-8302, 8315-8334). Two kinds of Arrow. (L VIII.-10. 6923 rev. and 6924 rev., note). Arrow heads, etc. (L VIII.-1. 6086 and 6087).

entries.title: The monster ǁkhwai-hemm's speech to the Mantis and the Mantis's reply
entries.snippet: The monster ||khwai-hemm's speech and |kaggen's (the Mantis's) reply, in which |kaggen speaks of the monster devouring |kaggen's things and the Porcupine's things, without waiting for the food that |kaggen will give…

entries.title: The Moon
entries.snippet: About the Moon and death; good and bad moons; the stars also know things the people do not understand and the Moon sees things that will come to pass; if it is looked at, the Moon can allow game to live, even if the…

entries.title: The Moon and Sun (another version of The Sun which pierces the Moon with its knife by ǁkabbo)
entries.snippet: The waxing and waning of the Moon is described as well as the rising and setting of the Sun who stabs the full Moon with its knife.

entries.title: The Moon and the Hare (origin of death)
entries.snippet: The Hottentot myth of the Origin of Death is also found in Bushman; but the Bushman form of it first related to us is very different from the Hottentot, be it more or less original. We have two versions of it.

entries.title: The Moon becomes angry at the children's laughing at him: an explanation of the eclipse of the moon
entries.snippet: The Moon becomes angry if people laugh at it, and goes into the sky (i.e. becomes eclipsed). When its anger has cooled, and its heart is 'comfortable' again, it comes out (L II.-35. 3154 rev.-3156 rev.).

entries.title: The Moon not to be laughed at
entries.snippet: The Moon is not to be laughed at. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII--28. 8441-8443, 8446). People say that the Moon becomes angry when they laugh at it.

entries.title: The Moon seeking his wife, ǁko'on
entries.snippet: The Moon in search of his wife. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-28. 8443-8445). The Moon returns home and finds that his wife ||ko'on is not there.

entries.title: The Moon, not to be looked at, when game is shot
entries.snippet: The Moon is not to be looked at, when game has been shot, for fear of evil consequences. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (LV.-21. 5643-5654).

entries.title: The name of a star in the Katkop dialect: the Hare's star
entries.snippet: The names of a number of Stars have been ascertained with the kind help of Mr. Maclear. Besides a separate alphabetical index to the Stars, the names of some are given in a supplementary list (B XXV.

entries.title: The names of a few places in Bushmanland, told to me long ago by Jantje Tooren and put in here for safety
entries.snippet: The names of a few places in Bushmanland found on an insert in the notebook.

entries.title: The names of animals
entries.snippet: Names given to animals by the |xam, including notes on who uses them (men or women, children and so on).

entries.title: The names of stones
entries.snippet: A list of the |xam and proper names of stones and gemstones, given by Dia!kwain at the Philadelphia Exhibition. The Bushman names for a few Stones were given by Díä!kwãin (L V.-20.

entries.title: The names of ǀkaggen's wife, son and daughter
entries.snippet: The names of the wife, son, and daughter of the Mantis. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-1. 6137 rev.).

entries.title: The new maiden who ate the ostrich marrow from the thigh-bone of the ostrich without the knowledge of her people
entries.snippet: A new maiden secretly eats the marrow from inside the ostrich's thigh-bone, although she is not supposed to.

entries.title: The new maiden who threw !huin into the sky; which became stars, and the wood ashes which were upon it, the Milky Way
entries.snippet: A Girl being angry with her mother, because the latter asked her for a certain kind of food which she had put to roast in the fire, threw it with the wood-ashes that were upon it, into the air.

entries.title: The north wind
entries.snippet: Wind and Weather. The Four Winds. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-1. 6096-6101). The north wind blows underneath the rain-clouds and makes them strong.

entries.title: The occasion on which the story The girl of the Early Race who killed the children of the Rain was related to ǀhanǂkass'o
entries.snippet: |hanǂkass'o was told the story 'The girl of the Early Race who killed the children of the Rain' when, as a child, he complained about being given too little to eat.

entries.title: The old clay pot
entries.snippet: The making of Clay Pots. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-23. 8054-8067). Fragments of Pottery. (L VIII.-27. 8436 and 8437). |hanǂkass'o describes and identifies a broken clay pot.

entries.title: The old man's song (written again separately, from ǂgirri-sse's dictation)
entries.snippet: The Old Man, the Little Hare, and the Lions. Related by ǂgiri-sse (L VII.-1. 6032-6040, partly translated). A fragment of the above story was also related by ǂgiri-sse (L VII.-1. 6031).

entries.title: The old woman and the hyena (ǀa!kunta's version which he heard from his mother)
entries.snippet: An old woman who was too weak to walk, was left behind by her people, when they, to escape starvation, travelled to another part of the country.

entries.title: The old woman and the hyena (ǁkabbo's version)
entries.snippet: An old woman who was too weak to walk was left behind by her people when they, to escape starvation, travelled to another part of the country.

entries.title: The old woman's song
entries.snippet: The old woman sings about the Hyena who carried her off, and how she sprang aside when he tried to throw her onto a pointed rock, so that he instead fell onto the pointed rock and was killed.

entries.title: The origin of the Moon
entries.snippet: The origin of the Moon is an episode in the story of the Mantis and his pet eland.

entries.title: The owl and the black crow
entries.snippet: The Owl and the Black Crow foretell the approach of the Lion. By Dia!kwãin (L V.-9. 4689-4706 1/2, 4696 rev., and 4697 rev.).

entries.title: The personal histories of various people
entries.snippet: Names of various Bushmen and Bushman women, with information regarding them.-Partly in Bushman by and partly in English after lhanǂkass'o. (L VIII.-1. 6078-6081, 4. 6370 rev., Note, 11. 6969 rev., Note 14.

entries.title: The Phyllomorpha paradoxa (or withered-leaf insect)
entries.snippet: A game played with the Phyllomorpha paradoxa or 'Withered Leaf' Insect. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-9. 6775-6785).

entries.title: The place to which people go after death, and various ways of dying and being killed
entries.snippet: Death. The place to which the Bushmen go after death. The various ways of dying, and of being killed (L II.-12. 1173-1243, 13. 1244-1314. 14. 1315-1396).

entries.title: The porcupine eats ǁkuarri
entries.snippet: A note on how ||kuarri is eaten by porcupines, but is poisonous to people.

entries.title: The power of cutting possessed by the reed and quartz
entries.snippet: Stone-knives. By /hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-27. 8434). Reed and stone used for cutting purposes. (L VIII.-26. 8313 and 8314. The power of cutting possessed by a reed and by quartz. (L. VII.-21. 7826 rev. and 7827 rev., note).

entries.title: The power over ostriches possessed by Dia!kwain's uncle, ǀuherre or 'Blaitje'
entries.snippet: The power over ostriches possessed by /uhérre (maternal uncle of Díä!kwãin). This is followed by a long discourse concerning successful and unsuccessful hunting, as well as by a prayer, addressed by the mother of the…

entries.title: The preparation of feather brushes used in springbok hunting
entries.snippet: How the Feather Brushes used in springbok hunting are prepared and smoked. By /hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-23. 8073-8075, 8083 1/2 and 8083 1/2 rev.).

entries.title: The preparation of the drum; ears of springbok are tied to the feet of the men who dance
entries.snippet: The Bushman drum, and dancing-rattles. Mode of preparation of the latter, by the women. By |hanǂkass'o, (L VIII.-1. 6127-6137). The drum is prepared by pulling springbok skin over a pot's mouth.

entries.title: The preparation of the springbok's ears
entries.snippet: The Bushman drum, and dancing-rattles. Mode of preparation of the latter, by the women. By |hanǂkass'o, (L VIII.-1. 6127-6137).

entries.title: The preparation of tinder from a certain thorn tree
entries.snippet: The preparation of tinder. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.10. 4761 rev.4764 rev., note). Tinder-making, by |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-28. 8472 and 8473).

entries.title: The quagga is also a wind's thing
entries.snippet: The quagga is wild and runs and is also a 'wind's thing'.

entries.title: The Quagga who was poisoned by her husband, !kuin'ssi-ǀkauoken
entries.snippet: The Quagga, who was poisoned by her husband, !kuinssi/kauöken. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-29. 8603-8614, 30. 8615-8627).

entries.title: The Quagga's story
entries.snippet: The Quagga, who fed her hungry child with a piece of her own liver, and whose death was caused by her husband, the Jackal, who put poisoned pieces of sharp bone into her bed, because his relations said that he had…

entries.title: The rain (and eating tortoises)
entries.snippet: Certain serpents and tortoises supposed to be kept in store by the Rain. (L VIII.-16. 7431 rev. and 7432 rev.).

entries.title: The rain sorcerer ǁkunn; two of ǁkunn's children
entries.snippet: Information regarding ǁkunn was also given by |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-20. 7746-7749). |hanǂkass'o speaks of the rainmaker, ||kunn, (who he observed making rain fall) and his relations.

entries.title: The Rain, in the form of an eland, shot by one of the Early Race of people; and the disasters which followed
entries.snippet: The Rain, in the form of an Eland, shot by one of the early race of people (which preceded the Bushmen in their country). The disasters which follow. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-16. 7461 and 7462, 17. 7463-7472).

entries.title: The Rain's story, and ǀkannu the waterhole
entries.snippet: A note has been made, from ǁkábbo's information, of a story not yet written down in Bushman, telling how the Rain carried off a girl belonging to the First Bushmen (or the ancient people living before the Bushmen), who…

entries.title: The rainmaker ǁkunn
entries.snippet: The Rain Sorcerer, ||kunn. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-22. 5743-5754). ||kunn says that the people have spoken harshly to him, as if he really owned the rain.

entries.title: The Ratel and the girls of the Early Race
entries.snippet: The Ratel (formerly a man of the Early Race (or !xwe ||na ss'o !kui) dances for the girls of the Early Race, who clap for him to continue dancing all day long.

entries.title: The reason why the ostrich does not click
entries.snippet: The reason why the Ostrich is represented as speaking without making use of click. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-30. 8628 rev. and 8629 rev., note).

entries.title: The reasons for the colours of the gemsbok, the hartebeest, the eland, the quagga, and the springbok
entries.snippet: To this myth [Mantis and the eland, piercing of the bladder] belongs also the account of the reasons for the colours of the gemsbok, hartebeest, eland, quagga, and springbok, given in the Katkop dialect by Día!kwãin (L…

entries.title: The scene of !kaua doro and the lion
entries.snippet: [The recto pages are not translated] The place the story occurred was shown to |hanǂkass'o as a child by a man called !kou (it was his place), who also told him the name of the place (which sounded like…

entries.title: The She-Rhinoceros and her elder daughter's suitors
entries.snippet: The Rhinoceros and her daughter's suitors. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-5. 6456-6504). The She-Rhinoceros has two daughters.

entries.title: The slaying of a white springbok will cause the other springbok to disappear
entries.snippet: The people of |hanǂkass'o's country do not kill a white springbok, because they believe that the other springbok will disappear.

entries.title: The son of the Wind
entries.snippet: The Son of the Wind. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-8. 6687-6708). |na ka ti's mother tells him not to say the name of the son of the Wind until his father has made a hut for them.

entries.title: The song of the kwa kwara, or korhaan malkop
entries.snippet: The song sung by the korhaan malkop, or kwa kwara (also known as the bustard, the black koran or Eupodotis afra). It sings of the Early Times when its head was burnt in the fire.

entries.title: The song of the Mother Rhinoceros
entries.snippet: The song of the Mother Rhinoceros (not translated), accompanied by notes on movements performed in the singing and dancing of it.

entries.title: The song of the Springbok mothers
entries.snippet: What the Springbok mothers sing when they are soothing their Springbok children.

entries.title: The song of the young woman, as she returned home
entries.snippet: The Young Woman of the early race whose breast was caught in a cleft of the rock. Her escape from the Two Lions. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-32. 8821-8842).

entries.title: The south wind
entries.snippet: Wind and Weather. The Four Winds. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-1. 6096-6101). The south wind blows, 'standing above', and 'makes dust'.

entries.title: The springbok resemble the water of the sea
entries.snippet: The springbok resemble the water of the sea. There are so many of them the Dutch ('Boers') soon exhaust their ammunition shooting them.

entries.title: The springbok's story
entries.snippet: Springbok will lead people to a lion. Their knowledge of distant things gained by their fine scent. In the Katkop dialect, by Dia!kwãin, from his parents (L V.-9. 4619-4651).

entries.title: The story of !gwa !nuntu and the Elephants
entries.snippet: The !gwá-!nuntu, whose grandchild is carried off by Elephants, and recovered by himself. By /hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-4. 6334-6413, 5. 6414-6455).

entries.title: The story of !khwe-ǀna ssho-!kui: the man who took a young Lion, and made use of it as a dog ( a story of the First Bushman)
entries.snippet: One of the ancient people preceding the Bushmen brought home a lion's cub, telling his wife that it was a dog which his younger brother had given him, and that it was the whelp of a dog which killed gemsbok.

entries.title: The story of !ko'-g !nuin-tara, or, !ko'-g !nuin-tara and the Day's Heart star
entries.snippet: The Story of !kó-g!nuin-tára, wife of the 'Dawn's-Heart' Star. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.--7. 8393-8432).

entries.title: The story of a sorceress; that which she did when the Kafir had hurt my throat, she 'snored' me, when my throat was swollen
entries.snippet: The Bushman Doctor or Sorcerer (L II-1. 273-275). An account of these people was also given in the Katkop dialect by Dia!kwáin (L V.-3. 4122-4131), from whom we besides have two reports of cures performed upon himself.

entries.title: The story of the Hyena (the Anteater's laws)
entries.snippet: This account [of The Anteater, Springbok and Lynx] is again followed by the Anteater's laws concerning the habits of various animals, such as the jackal, the 'Löffelhund' (Proteles), etc., who were, once upon a time,…

entries.title: The story of the Hyena and the Lion (2nd version)
entries.snippet: Not only in the astrological mythology, but also in the world of Bushman Fable, the lion naturally occupies a prominent position.

entries.title: The story of the Jackal (the Anteater's laws)
entries.snippet: This account [of The Anteater, Springbok and Lynx] is again followed by the Anteater's laws concerning the habits of various animals, such as the jackal, the 'Löffelhund' (Proteles), etc., who were, once upon a time,…

entries.title: The story of the Kwa-kkwara
entries.snippet: The Knorhaan (Brandkop) who married his sister. Told to Dia!kwãin by his mother (L V.-5. 4292-4320). Apparently a version of the fable imbedded in the Anteater's Laws.

entries.title: The story of the Leopard Tortoise
entries.snippet: The story of the Great Water Tortoise, who deceived men belonging to the early race of Bushman by feigning illness (told by !kwéiten ta ǁken), appears to be a variation of the above-mentioned myth (L VI.-2. 4055-4063).

entries.title: The story of the Lions and the Ostriches
entries.snippet: The Lion jealous of the voice of the Ostrich. In the Katkop dialect by Dia!kwãin, who heard it from his paternal grandfather, (L V.-5. 4320-4344).

entries.title: The story of the Lynx and the Anteater
entries.snippet: The Anteater, and its handsome musical pet, the Lynx. Related to Dia!kwãin by his paternal grandmother Ttwóbboken !kauken (L V--6. 4345-4377). The Lynx plays the !kummi so well that everyone loves her.

entries.title: The story of the Mantis and the Ostrich who talks (ǀkaggen and !kaken-!kaka-!k'aui)
entries.snippet: The account of the Mantis when he takes away the eggs of a certain fabulous bird, named !káken!káka káuï, is very curious.

entries.title: The story of the old man who makes rain
entries.snippet: The Rain-maker is asked to milk a nice female Rain which is gentle, the rain-clouds being her hair (L II.-24. 2213-2226, 25. 2227-2263).

entries.title: The story of the Ratel (or Mellivora) and the Waterskilpad
entries.snippet: The vicious Water-tortoise and the charitable Ratel (Mellivora), a fable related to Dia!kwãin, by his mother (L V.-5. 4265-4291).

entries.title: The story of the Silver Jackal (the Anteater's laws)
entries.snippet: This account [of The Anteater, Springbok and Lynx] is again followed by the Anteater's laws concerning the habits of various animals, such as the jackal, the 'Löffelhund' (Proteles), etc., who were, once upon a time,…

entries.title: The story of the Springbok's kid, who was carried off by the Elephants (an incomplete account)
entries.snippet: The pet springbok of the Mantis carried off by an Elephant, while the Mantis is in a hole, digging out sweet food for it,-the Elephant substitutes her own calf, the inarticulate reply of the latter to the questions of…

entries.title: The story of the Strandwolf and the Aardwolf and how they each marry their own kind (the Anteater's laws)
entries.snippet: This account [of The Anteater, Springbok and Lynx] is again followed by the Anteater's laws concerning the habits of various animals, such as the jackal, the 'Löffelhund' (Proteles), etc., who were, once upon a time,…

entries.title: The story of the widow of the man killed while hunting, and her return to her own family or The widow's story
entries.snippet: The widow of the wounded man returns to her own family with her children. Various customs after death are explained, as well as what happens to widows (they have no-one to hunt for them and eat 'old' or 'bad' things and…

entries.title: The story of Tssi-!kuara ǀhin (the Lioness and her adopted daughter)
entries.snippet: The Lioness and her adopted daughter, Tssi-!kuára-Ihin. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-25. 8084-8169, 8171 and 8172, 25. 8173-8176).
entries.date_label: 9 January 1879 (started, and finished on or about the 19 January)

entries.title: The story of ǀgwai (who killed his sister-in-law, and was killed by his brother-in-law)
entries.snippet: |gwai (a person who was formerly a beast of prey and a man of the Early Race) kills his sister-in-law and his brother-in-law is angry with him and seeks revenge (the title implies that |gwai is caught and killed but it…

entries.title: The story of ǀkuken-ǀu ǀunu
entries.snippet: The story of |kuken-|u |unu (a caterpillar, larva of Aloa) who was formerly a person of the Early Race. |kuken-|u |unu curses a girl and she falls ill.

entries.title: The Sun pierces the full Moon with his knife
entries.snippet: Whilst in the preceding myths of the Mantis, the Moon, according to its origin, is only a piece of leather (a shoe of the Mantis), in Bushman astrological mythology the Moon is looked upon as a man who incurs the wrath…

entries.title: The tale of a wise person or sorceress, what she said, when she talked with us
entries.snippet: The Bushman Doctor or Sorcerer (L II.-1. 273-275). An account of these people was also given in the Katkop dialect by Dia!kwáin (L V.-3. 4122-4131), from whom we besides have two reports of cures performed upon himself.

entries.title: The telephonus (!koroken !koroken)
entries.snippet: The !kóroken-!kóroken (Telophonus . . .) must not be mocked by the Bushman children. The result of so doing described. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-25. 6014-6024).

entries.title: The treatment of the 'growing' girl
entries.snippet: ||kabbo describes the seclusion of the 'growing' (or menstruating) girl in a hut with a tiny door by her mother: she has to lookeep her eyes on the ground when she leaves the hut and cannot go far.

entries.title: The Ttu ttutten (birds)
entries.snippet: The ttu ttutten make a nest that takes up an entire tree, although the entrance to the nest is small, as the ttu ttutten are small birds (they are the 'children of smallness').

entries.title: The two Lions, the Lizards, the Blue Crane, the Rhebuck, and the Black Crow
entries.snippet: The Two Lions, the Lizards, the Blue Crane, the Rhebok and the Crow. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-32. 8859-8878, 8848-8852, 19. 7643-7656). Two Lions (!gu and his companion, !haue ta ǂhou) kill the lizards !khau and |gi.

entries.title: The two Lions: pointers to the Southern Cross
entries.snippet: The names of the two lions are !gu and !háuë ta ǂhóu. There are four birds mentioned in connection with them, the /gi and his wife the /ki, the !kao and his wife the !k'o (or 'Blue Crain').

entries.title: The use of the !goin-!goin, followed by an account of a Bushman dance
entries.snippet: The employment of the !gõin-!gõin (the !gõin-!gõin consists of a [variously-sized] blade of wood, attached by a little cord to a short stick.

entries.title: The Vultures and their elder sister
entries.snippet: The Vultures, their elder sister (who was a girl of the early race), and her husband. By/|hanǂkass'o (LVIII.-27. 8351-8373).

entries.title: The Water's story: more about how maidens adorn young men
entries.snippet: What a Maiden must eat and avoid (L II.-28. 2520-2524). How a New Maiden is treated, has been told in the Katkop dialect, by !kwéiten ta ||ken (L VI.-2.

entries.title: The west wind
entries.snippet: Wind and Weather. The Four Winds. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-1. 6096-6101). The west wind turns back the rain-clouds and sends them to the east.

entries.title: The Wildebeest, the Mice, the Quaggas and the Mantis, or why the (black) Wildebeest has a white or light coloured tail
entries.snippet: The Wildebeest, the Mice, the Quaggas and the Mantis. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-30. 8651-8667).

entries.title: The Wind
entries.snippet: The Wind was formerly a man, but is now a bird, and lives in the mountain. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-8. 6709-6713, and 6694 rev., note).

entries.title: The woman eaten by Baboons: a fragment of the account
entries.snippet: In former times, when animals were people, the Baboons were jealous of a young Quagga woman, who lived with them, on account of the notice attracted by her great size.

entries.title: The woman who was killed by the Baboons
entries.snippet: In former times, when animals were people, the Baboons were jealous of a young Quagga woman, who lived with them, on account of the notice attracted by her great size.

entries.title: The Xara and the Ichneumon
entries.snippet: A story of the Xara (or Erdmann) and his fight with Ichneumon, who wants to take the Xara's eland, so picks a fight but loses and runs away.

entries.title: The young man who was changed into stone, by the glance of a new maiden
entries.snippet: A youth climbs up a mountain playing his !goura. A new maiden lying in her hut wants to see the youth play and looks straight at him. The youth is turned to stone with the !goura still in his mouth.

entries.title: The young woman who disobeyed her mother, and fell in with the two Lions (!haue ta ǂhou and !gu)
entries.snippet: The Young Woman of the early race whose breast was caught in a cleft of the rock. Her escape from the Two Lions. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-32. 8821-8842).

entries.title: The youth (of the Early Race of people) who warned those at home of the approach of a Koranna commando
entries.snippet: The youth of the early race, who saved the lives of the people at home by warning them of the approach of a Koranna war party. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-25. 8251-8261, 26. 8262-8268).

entries.title: The ǀgoo or ǂgebbi-gu
entries.snippet: The ǂgebbi-ggu, as performed among the Grass Bushmen, was described by /hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-22. 7978 and 7979). A description of the /goo, or the ǂgebbi-gu, dance.

entries.title: The ǀka kau: a little bird said by the Bushmen, by whom it is not eaten, to laugh at the wildcat
entries.snippet: About the |ka kau bird, which laughs at the wild cat and is not eaten by people.

entries.title: The ǀkaggen who took ǀkammanga's shoe, and turned it into an eland
entries.snippet: In the arrangement of the myths regarding the Mantis, it has appeared to me most convenient to place first that one in which the Mantis takes away a shoe belonging to his son-in-law /kwámmana, and converts it into an…

entries.title: The ǀkain ǀkain, the girls and the Mantis
entries.snippet: The |kain |kain bird stabs a girl in the breast when she lies ill. The |kain |kain rubs her blood on his nose and goes to another girl.

entries.title: The ǀkhu or Bushman soup-spoon
entries.snippet: The /khu, or Bushman soup spoon. This is a brush of native manufacture, the stem of which is also used to scratch the fire together. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-1. 6083 and 6084).

entries.title: The ǀkhuken-ǀu ǀunu
entries.snippet: About the |khuken-|u |unu, which is a rain's 'thing' (or !khoa ka ||kerri-ssi !kau).

entries.title: The ǀßkururu (ǀxabbe, or 'Kritje')
entries.snippet: The Grillus Capensis, or 'Kritje', [or |ßkururu] roasted and mixed with puffader poison and so-called 'boomgift'. In the Katkop dialect, by ǂkásin (L IV.-2. 3516-3519).

entries.title: The ǁkauru-opua, or little water-hole found in rock or stone
entries.snippet: The ||kauru-opua, or a small water-hole, is found in flat stones on the ground.

entries.title: The ǁkerri (locust bird)
entries.snippet: [not translated]

entries.title: The ǁkerri bird and locusts
entries.snippet: The ||kerri bird drives locusts into bushes while the sun is shining, which they do not normally do.

entries.title: The ǁkerri: a bird which eats locusts; and locust-hunting
entries.snippet: The ||kerri bird eats locusts and is thus called the locust-bird. They drive the locusts into the bushes, where the people collect them and roast them in a fire.

entries.title: The ǁkuken
entries.snippet: The people take a long ostrich wing feather and cut off the root to make a ǁkuken, or barblet, that makes the arrow stick fast in an animal's flesh.

entries.title: The ǁkuken and ostrich feathers
entries.snippet: The name of the barblet (||kuken) on the stems of arrows that catch in the flesh of the shot animal.

entries.title: The ǂnuturu
entries.snippet: ǂnuturu dances for the children and sings to herself until the people return. She takes off her 'face's horns' and the people see that her face underneath is white and beautiful.

entries.title: The ǃau or shaped rib-bone used by Bushmen in eating some kinds of food
entries.snippet: The !ãù, or shaped rib-bone, used by Bushmen in eating certain food. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-1. 6082). The facing page contains a note as to how it is shaped or 'worked'.

entries.title: Things girls and youths must avoid (the rain's things)
entries.snippet: What Maidens (and young men) must not say or do, to avoid the wrath of the Water. By Dia!kwãin (L V.-6. 4377 rev.-4407 rev.).

entries.title: Throwing stones at locusts
entries.snippet: The locusts (with the locust birds which accompany them) are set free by Sorcerers. Stones not to be thrown at the locusts. (L V.-21. 5708-5719 1/2) (L VIII.-31. 8754-8758).

entries.title: Thy name which is a Bushman name, what is it?'
entries.snippet: Inquiry regarding name and dwelling-place. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-19. 5448-5452). Dia!kwain longs to hear the words of his own language.

entries.title: Told in illustration of the picture of the dance of sorcerers (Mr Stow's number 8 and 9)
entries.snippet: Remarks upon sorcerers, their dress, etc. (in explanation of one of Mr Stow's copies of Bushmen paintings), by Dia!kwãin (L V.-10. 4744-4750). The dance of sorcerers is called the ||ken.

entries.title: Treatment of bones
entries.snippet: Porcupine's bones are gathered together and placed on an ostrich breastbone and pouredopposite the hut's entrance.

entries.title: Tsatsi
entries.snippet: About |hanǂkass'o's maternal grandfather Tsatsi (Tsa t'ssi), who was a passionate man (which is why he was never a 'sorcerer' or 'doctor'). He only ever listened to |hanǂkass'o's mother.

entries.title: Tsatsi's treatment of bones
entries.snippet: |hanǂkass'o's grandfather Tsatsi puts different kinds of bones away in the sticks of the hut to avoid wounding the first finger of his right hand.

entries.title: Tto, 'rooi klip' (how tto is obtained)
entries.snippet: The Bushmen anoint their heads with ǁhára. (which is black and sparkling), mixed with fat, and rub their bodies with ttò. (L VIII.-14. 7272 rev.,-7276 rev., note). Ttò or 'Roode Klip' is dug out from the mountain side.

entries.title: Unsuccessful springbok hunting after death of companion (means employed to make it more fortunate)
entries.snippet: The hunter is unable to kill a springbok if his companion has been killed. His arrows miss the springbok and if an arrow does hit the springbok it runs on as if not wounded.

entries.title: Various foods and the protection of the rain for a fungus
entries.snippet: !haken, a food resembling 'Bushman rice' is also used by Bushmen. (L VIII.-9. 6789 rev., note). That !haken above which a certain fungus grows is given to the old people. (L VIII.-11. 6945 rev.-6954 rev.).

entries.title: Visit to Dr Stewart
entries.snippet: ǁkábbo's visit to Dr. P. G. Stewart, to be vaccinated. (L II.-1. 275-277).

entries.title: Want of rain
entries.snippet: Drought. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-1. 6102-6106). When the west wind turns back the rain-clouds there is a drought or 'want' of rain and the people are thirsty and have to travel far to fetch water.

entries.title: We do not utter a star man's name
entries.snippet: Certain names not to be uttered. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-23. 8071 rev.). |hanǂkass'o's grandfather (Tsatsi) told him that he should never say a star man's name (or a star's man's name).

entries.title: What bow strings are made of
entries.bleek_lloyd_ref: not numbered, an insert in Book A2_1_24
entries.snippet: A note on what bow strings are made of. Lloyd notes that she cannot be sure if these are for shooting or playing. Different sinews are listed.

entries.title: What happened when the thong (with which they were pulling at the Water's Bull) broke or Kko-kkoro's story
entries.snippet: An account of rain-making by means of dragging a water-animal over the land, in illustration of a Bushman painting copied by Mr J.M.

entries.title: What happens when we die
entries.snippet: The relations of wind, moon, and cloud to human beings after death, etc. (L V.-15. 5147-5158). What happens when the |xam die.

entries.title: What is done by the Bushmen (men and women) in an eclipse of the sun
entries.snippet: What Bushmen do when an eclipse of the sun takes place. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-28. 8438-8441). They strike sticks together, as well as shoes, to make the sun come out.

entries.title: What is done with a 'new' maiden
entries.snippet: What a Maiden must eat and avoid (L II.28. 2520-2524). How a New Maiden is treated, has been told in the Katkop dialect, by !kwéiten ta ||ken (L VI.-2.

entries.title: What is said to a person whose actions are disagreeable
entries.snippet: What is said to people whose actions are disagreeable (or 'not nice'). The person is cursed and others are angry with him. The people question his treatment of them.

entries.title: What sorcerers eat
entries.snippet: Some sorcerers are said to eat the flesh of the dead. (L VIII.-15. 7304-7306). |hanǂkass'o is told by various !giten (sorcerers) whom he knows that some (bad) !giten eat people.

entries.title: What the 'Bushman rice' does when the ǁxo hai stars come out
entries.snippet: What the so-called 'Bushman-rice' (i.e. larvae of the ants) does, when the star Altair comes out (L II.-37. 3344-3347).

entries.title: What the children say to the Moon as it rises making it angry
entries.snippet: Added to this are the words of derision sometimes addressed, by Bushman children, to the Moon as it rises, making it angry (L II-35. 3158 rev.).

entries.title: What the lion did to Xwerri-kau; and what parts of game should not be eaten by little children
entries.snippet: A full-grown Bushman, whom the narrator knew, punished by a lion for having eaten forbidden portions of the lion's prey, as a child. Told by Día!kwãin (L V.-8. 4574-4617 1/2, 9. 4618, with notes upon 4616 rev.

entries.title: What the man did to his wife when she was pregnant
entries.snippet: ... A man cut his wife open, because he believed that she had been greedy, and then discovering, with great distress, that she was with child, tries in vain to repair the harm he had done, by pinning her together with a…

entries.title: What the man does and says to the new Ssho ǀoa so that it may know him
entries.snippet: When the man digs up the new Ssho |oa, he bites off and chews a small piece of the root and spits out the saliva into a hole in the ground with the plant still in it so that it may 'know' him.

entries.title: What the owl says
entries.snippet: What the owl says.

entries.title: What the people eat and wear (the Anteater's laws) (ǁkabbo's version 2)
entries.snippet: Descriptions of what the |xam hunt, eat and wear as clothes and shoes and skins. The Anteater told the people to cook meat on a fire and use pots and other utensils, whereas other animals eat raw flesh.

entries.title: What the springbok and the gemsbok did when they knew that Dia!kwain's wife would die
entries.snippet: Death of the narrator's first wife, which was foretold by springbok and gemsbok. By Dia!kwãin (L V.-9. 4653-4688). An account of what the springbok did when Dia!kwain's first wife (Mietje) died.

entries.title: What the stars say
entries.snippet: The stars call out 'Tsau! Tsau!'. The people say that the stars are cursing the springbok's eyes for them.

entries.title: What Xaa-ttin used to sing (the broken string)
entries.snippet: The sorcerer !nuin|kúïten. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-15. 5079-5101, 5104-5109).

entries.title: What ǀkaggen does when an eland has been shot
entries.bleek_lloyd_ref: Insert inside the front cover of Book II-36
entries.snippet: When an eland has been shot, |kaggen (the Mantis) buzzes in the hunters' ears, stands on their eyebrows and looks in their quivers to find the 'offending' arrow.

entries.title: What ǁkabbo said about his intended return home to Bushmanland
entries.snippet: ǁkábbo's intended return home. He awaits the Moon that he may return, and hear the Bushman stories. Here he works woman's work, while his comrades at home hear histories that travel; but he does not visit, as they do.

entries.title: When a good-looking person is ill
entries.snippet: The handsome child, who is ill, is not to be sent to fetch water. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-15. 7296-7298). When a 'good-looking' or handsome person is ill he is taken care of because of his appearance.

entries.title: When Bushmen were springbucks and cried
entries.snippet: In the Early Times, when people were springboks, they cried like children when |kaggen (the Mantis) shot them. |kaggen said they should become people because of how they cried.

entries.title: Why !kweiten ta ǁken received her name
entries.snippet: Explanation of various personal names was given, in the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-10. 4751-4754, 4758-4777, 18. 5373 rev., 19. 5505 rev. and 5506 rev., 20. 5572 and 5573, 5605-5607).

entries.title: Why Dia!kwain's brother Ko-bbo received his name
entries.snippet: Explanation of various personal names was given, in the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-10. 4751-4754, 4758-4777, 18. 5373 rev., 19. 5505 rev. and 5506 rev., 20. 5572 and 5573, 5605-5607).

entries.title: Why Dia!kwain's uncle ǀkai kwa received his name
entries.snippet: Explanation of various personal names was given, in the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-10. 4751-4754, 4758-4777, 18. 5373 rev., 19. 5505 rev. and 5506 rev., 20. 5572 and 5573, 5605-5607).

entries.title: Why the chameleon must not be killed
entries.snippet: ...reasons for not killing the Chameleon, which came out of the sky (L IV.-3. p.iv.).

entries.title: Wildcat turns into a lion
entries.snippet: The Wild Cat believed to possess the power of turning itself into a lion. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-23. 8080-8083, and 27. 8399 rev., note).

entries.title: Wind and stars
entries.snippet: The winds which are supposed to appertain to different persons. (L VIII.-28. 8459-8465). After the wind has blown and dies, the place becomes warm from the sunshine (its 'eye' is warm).

entries.title: Wind, weather and springbok hunting
entries.snippet: A story about the wind and the rain and the hunting of springbok. People who live facing the rain get their weapons wet, causing bowstrings to break.

entries.title: Windmaking and springbok hunting
entries.snippet: Wind-making; followed by a long discourse upon springbok hunting. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-8. 6725-6769 rev.).

entries.title: Words and sentences
entries.snippet: Words and sentences given by |a!kunta (Stoffel). Included are copies, translated in January 1871, of some of the material collected in Lloyd's first notebooks.

entries.title: Words and sentences
Words and sentences
entries.snippet: A list of words and sentences - mostly body parts and numerals.

entries.title: Words and sentences
entries.snippet: Words and sentences given by |hanǂkass'o.

entries.title: Words and sentences
entries.snippet: |xam words and sentences, which appear to refer to parts of the body. Names of portions of the body of an animal. By lhanǂkass'o (L VIII.-31. 8774, 8772 rev. and 8773 rev.).

entries.title: Words and sentences
entries.snippet: Miscellaneous words and sentences given by |hanǂkass'o.

entries.title: Words and sentences (and Dia!kwain's father's drawings)
entries.bleek_lloyd_ref: inserts on the inside cover of Book V-17
entries.snippet: A series of six inserts on the inside cover of Book V-17 which contain brief references to falling stars, earthquakes and sorcerers, as well as some |xam names for certain animals.

entries.title: Words and sentences (including the names of stars)
entries.snippet: Words and sentences with many entries concerning the names of celestial bodies and the heavens. p.199v features a note on the great star, which follows daybreak (the Day's Heart star, or Jupiter).

entries.title: Words and sentences given by ǀhanǂkass'o
entries.snippet: Some names of animals and other things from daily life given by |hanǂkass'o. Names of Animals (L VIII.-1. 6047-6049, 21. 7828-7830, 7834, 7836-7838, 7841, 23. 8068 rev., 8070 rev., 25. 8260 rev., 32.

entries.title: Words and sentences given by ǀhanǂkass'o at the South African Museum
entries.snippet: Names of Animals, mainly identified at the South African Museum. Given by |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-2. 6141-6145, 9. 6770-6775, 21. 7817, 7819-7821, 7842 and 7843, 7843, 7846, 31. 8790 rev. and 8791).

entries.title: Words and sentences given by ǀhanǂkass'o at the South African Museum
entries.snippet: Names of Animals, mainly identified at the South African Museum. Given by |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-2. 6141-6145, 9. 6770-6775, 21. 7817, 7819-7821, 7842 and 7843, 7843, 7846, 31. 8790 rev. and 8791).

entries.title: Words and sentences given by ǀhanǂkass'o: names of colours and patterns
entries.snippet: Names for Colours, etc. Given by |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-7. 6601, 6606 and 6605 rev., 24. 8170).

entries.title: Words and sentences given by ǀhanǂkass'o: parts of the body
entries.snippet: Words and Sentences given by |hanǂkass'o naming parts of the body. Parts of the body. By Man-tonno (L XXII. 10407-10413). Ditto.By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-1. 6059 and 6051, 7. 6598 rev.). Names for certain bones, etc.

entries.title: Words and sentences: Boer's [Dutch] names for sheep
entries.snippet: The Dutch names for and descriptions of sheep.

entries.title: Words and sentences: by !kweiten ta ǁken
