entries.snippet: Sentences on some |xam who visited !kweiten ta ||ken's husband ǂkasin, and where they come from. Words in the Katkop dialect, from ǂkásin (B XXV. 2355-2360), from !kwéiten ta ǁken (L VI.-1.

entries.title: Words and sentences: by !kweiten ta ǁken
entries.snippet: Words and sentences relating to sounds and music, given by !kweiten ta ||ken.

entries.title: Words and sentences: given by ǂgirri-sse
entries.snippet: Words and Sentences. Given by ǂgirri-sse (L VII.-1. 6041-6044). Includes the sentence 'I come from the strong water', meaning the Breakwater Convict Station.

entries.title: Words and sentences: given by ǂkasin
entries.snippet: Names of animals, mainly identified at the Museum (B II. 358 and 359, 366-368, XXV. 2341-2344, 2349 and 2350; L II.-2 288-291); further, by /a!kúnta (LI.-2. 184-188); and lastly in the Katkop dialect by ǂkásin (B XXV.

entries.title: Words and sentences: got at Breakwater
entries.bleek_lloyd_ref: 1-19, 20-39, 40-60, 61-118, page inside front cover of first Book (UCT_BC151A2_1_1)
entries.snippet: Words and sentences given by |a!kunta (Stoffel).

entries.title: Words and sentences: names and descriptions of animals, and a song
entries.snippet: The |xam (and Dutch) names of animals found in Bushmanland. The piece includes the eating and description of animals. A note on p.189 mentions a song of the |xam, 'which is sung to the !goura'.

entries.title: Words and sentences: names of animals
entries.snippet: The names of certain animals.

entries.title: Words and sentences: names of animals (at the South African Museum)
entries.snippet: Names of animals given at the South African Museum.

entries.title: Words and sentences: names of animals and other terms relating to daily life
entries.snippet: Words and sentences given by |hanǂkass'o naming certain animals and common terms.

entries.title: Words and sentences: names of bird's eggs
entries.snippet: Names of nine birds, with their descriptions written down in English, from some birds' eggs, not yet scientifically identified. (L II.-1. 182 and 183.)

entries.title: Words and sentences: names of bones
entries.snippet: Names for certain bones, etc. (L VIII.-16. 7401 and 7402). |xam names and descriptions of various bones and tendons (found in animals).

entries.title: Words and sentences: names of colours
entries.snippet: Names for Colours, etc. Given by |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-7. 6601, 6606 and 6605 rev., 24. 8170).

entries.title: Words and sentences: names of millipedes
entries.snippet: The names and some descriptions of millipedes and millipede-like insects.

entries.title: Words and sentences: parts of the body
entries.snippet: |xam names are given for parts of the body.

entries.title: Words and sentences: parts of the body
entries.snippet: Words in the Katkop dialect, from ǂkásin (B XXV. 2355-2360), from !kwéiten ta ||ken (L VI.-1. 3882, 3859-3897), and from Dia!kwãin (L V.-6. 4434 and 4435, 8. 4615 rev., 9. 4653 rev., etc.).

entries.title: Words and sentences: parts of the body
entries.snippet: Words in the Katkop dialect, from ǂkásin (B XXV. 2355-2360), from !kwéiten ta ǁken (L VI.-1. 3882, 3859-3897), and from Dia!kwãin (L V.-6. 4434 and 4435, 8. 4615 rev., 9. 4653 rev., etc.).

entries.title: Words and sentences: regarding the expressions ǀguobba ǁa, and !kwiten ǁa (as applied to the flight of bees)
entries.snippet: What people say when talking about the flight of bees.

entries.title: Words and sentences: terms for various relationships by marriage
entries.snippet: Terms for various relationships by marriage. (L VIII.-12. 7067 rev. and 7068 rev., 32. 8794 rev.).

entries.title: Words and sentences: the door is shut
entries.snippet: An expression used by the |xam.

entries.title: Words and sentences: various expressions for being angry
entries.snippet: Various expressions for being angry or comfortable with another person.

entries.title: Words and sentences: ǀxam names of animals
entries.bleek_lloyd_ref: inside front cover of Book VIII-21
entries.snippet: A list of |xam names of animals.

entries.title: Xaa-ttin's accident
entries.snippet: Explanation of various personal names was given, in the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-10. 4751-4754, 4758-4777, 18. 5373 rev., 19. 5505 rev. and 5506 rev., 20. 5572 and 5573, 5605-5607, translated).

entries.title: Xurri ko killed by a lion
entries.snippet: The death of a relative of the narrator from the bites of a lion, is told in the Katkop dialect by !kweiten ta ǁken (L VI.-2. 4010-4013).

entries.title: Young man of the Early Race put into a mouse skin and becomes a lion
entries.snippet: Children push a youth into the skin of a Fieldmouse and tell him to 'trot away', which he does, becoming a Lion. The children tell the Lion to go and lie down, which he does.

entries.title: Young unmarried women and girls must be silent and hide from the rain
entries.snippet: Young, unmarried women and girls must hide themselves from the rain. (L VII.-23. 8032 and 8031 rev.).

entries.title: ǀ a khumm called by a lion
entries.snippet: Another story explains how the lions talk as men, by putting their tails into their mouths. The sister of the narrator is said to have been thus addressed.

entries.title: ǀa!karaken killed by a lion
entries.snippet: A lion sits waiting for the sun to set so he can stalk people. The people lie down to sleep and the lion steals up on them and drags |a!karaken into the grove.

entries.title: ǀgui-an (Dootje) and her mistress, Trina de Klerk
entries.snippet: |hanǂkass'o tells the story of the abuse of |gui-an (or Doortje) who was beaten by her mistress, Trina de Klerck and also by Trina's husband Jacob.

entries.title: ǀhanǂkass'o's dream of a gang of prisoners
entries.snippet: |hanǂkass'o describes his dream of a group of prisoners. He mentions some of their names and describes the composition of the group.

entries.title: ǀharritan, the locust bird
entries.snippet: The |harriten bird is found in Bushmanland and resembles a crow.

entries.title: ǀkai ka !gaoken, a poison used by 'Grass Bushmen'
entries.bleek_lloyd_ref: not numbered (an insert inside front cover of A2_1_44)
entries.snippet: An insert in which Lloyd notes the making of a poison used, and feared, by 'Grass Bushmen'. It seems that grubs are used.

entries.title: ǀkannu the rainmaker
entries.snippet: ||kunnu who possessed locusts and rain, and was entreated for rain by a relative of the narrator. (L VIII.-7. 6639 rev. -6646 rev.).

entries.title: ǀku-te-!gaua and ǀkaggen
entries.snippet: /kú-te-!gaúä, who could enter fire unharmed, enables the Mantis to do the same. The ingratitude of the latter to his instructor again brings him into trouble; and his newly acquired power deserts him. (L VIII.-11.

entries.title: ǀkuamman-a, accompanied by the Mantis and the young Ichneumon, visits the house of the ǀku, or, The Mantis and the Proteles
entries.snippet: ... the account of a visit paid by /kwámmana, with a young Ichneumon and the Mantis, to the abode of the 'Löffelhund' (Proteles); where the Mantis again gets into trouble. (L VIII.1-6939-6944, 11. 6945-6977).

entries.title: ǀnu-!ke or magicians who have died still possess power, and ǂkamme-an's prayer to them
entries.snippet: The power of Game Sorcerers continues after death. Prayer addressed to them by the mother of narrator. (L V.-11. 4801 rev.-4809 rev.). Dia!kwain's mother tells of spirit-people who are dead game's sorcerers.

entries.title: ǀxam dialects ('Berg Bushmen' and 'River Bed people')
entries.snippet: Regarding some Bushman dialects. By lhanǂkass'o (L VIII.-19. 7672-7675). |hanǂkass'o comments on his understanding of other |xam dialects, spoken by 'Berg (Mountain) Bushmen' and 'River Bed people'.
entries.date_label: September 1878 or December 1879 (it is unknown which)

entries.title: ǀxam names
entries.snippet: Names of various Bushmen and Bushman women, with information regarding them. Partly in Bushman by and partly in English after |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-1. 6078-6081, 4. 6370 rev., note, 11. 6969 rev., note 14.

entries.title: ǀxannan ǀxannan
entries.snippet: Resemblance between /xannan-/xannan and an ostrich. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-23. 5858-5862). The son of /xannan-/xannan. (L V.-23. 5860 rev. and 5861 rev.).

entries.title: ǀxannan ǀxannan and the wind
entries.snippet: A certain old woman named /xannan-/xannan, who had power over the wind. In the Katkop dialect, by Díä!kwãin (L V.-23. 5842 rev., 5845, 5845 rev. and 5846 rev.). The protection extended by the Wind to /xannan-/xannan.

entries.title: ǁhara and Tto
entries.snippet: The Bushmen anoint their heads with ǁhára (which is black and sparkling), mixed with fat, and rub their bodies with ttò. (L VIII.-14. 7272 rev.,-7276 rev., note). Ttò or 'Roode Klip' is dug out from the mountain side.

entries.title: ǁkabbo ('Oud Jantje Tooren'), a 'Mantis's man'
entries.snippet: ||kábbo (father-in-law of /hanǂkass'o) was a Mantis's man (i.e. believed to possess these insects). By /hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-23. 8033).

entries.title: ǁkabbo tells of death, the hole, and the path of the First Bushmen
entries.snippet: ||kabbo tells of death: 'This path is a Bushman's path, the First Bushman's path, the Korannas, Boers, etc, also go on it.

entries.title: ǁkabbo's account of being caught and brought to the Breakwater
entries.snippet: ||kabbo's account of how he was taken by black men from the place where he was eating springbok, and was bound, along with some of his relations.

entries.title: ǁkabbo's account of being caught and jailed (2nd version)
entries.snippet: ǁkábbo's Capture and Journey to Cape Town are twice described by him. One of these accounts (L II.-1. 266-272), the beginning of which is also given in another version (B II.

entries.title: ǁkabbo's account of meeting with a lion
entries.snippet: ||kabbo's account of meeting a lion. While out walking in the veld, he sees a lion lying down and beats it with a stick on the head. The lion jumps up and runs away.

entries.title: ǁkabbo's dream of rain
entries.snippet: ǁkábbo's dreams of rain and their fulfilment. When the rain has fallen, ǁkábbo and his family store up water, and hunt springbok.

entries.title: ǁkabbo's maternal grandmother, who was a !gixa
entries.snippet: A short note about ||kabbo's maternal grandmother, who was a !gixa (or sorceress).

entries.title: ǁkabbo's place
entries.snippet: [A] note respecting sorcerers who assume the shape of birds or jackals, on p.4701 rev.).

entries.title: ǁkabbo's song on the loss of his tobacco pouch
entries.snippet: ||kabbo's song sung on the occasion of the theft of his tobacco pouch by the dog 'Blom'. It concerns his 'tobacco-hunger'.

entries.title: ǁkabbo's treatment of bones
entries.snippet: |hanǂkass'o's father-in-law, ||kabbo ('Dream' or Oud Jantje Tooren), throws bones away, and |han-kass'o does the same because he has married into the family.

entries.title: ǁkurru, or ǁkutten-ǁkutten (making weapons)
entries.snippet: Stone-knives: in English only, after ǂkásin (L IV.-1. 3481).

entries.title: ǁxuobbeten and the lion
entries.snippet: ||xuobbeten is carried away by the lion while she sleeps. It bites her head and kills her. It puts her in its mouth and carries her down to the water.

entries.title: ǂkagara and !haunu
entries.snippet: ǂkágára and !haunu, who fought each other with lightning. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-30 8637-8648).

entries.title: ǂkagara's fight with !haunu in the east
entries.snippet: See ǂkágára and !haunu, who fought each other with lightning. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII.-30 8637-8648).

entries.title: ǂkainyatara and the Ostrich
entries.snippet: The Ostrich who ran away with ǂkainyatára and gave him as a husband to her daughter. By |hanǂkass'o (L VIII. 30. 8628-8636). A story of the Early Race. The Ostrich snatches up ǂkainyatara and runs off with him.

entries.title: ǂkasin shoots a hyena
entries.snippet: ǂkasin follows his dogs to a hyena's hole. The dogs pull the hyena out and ǂkasin shoots it.

entries.title: ǂkasin's adventure with a leopard
entries.snippet: The narrator's adventure with a Leopard. In the Katkop dialect by ǂkásin (L IV.-1. 3458-3464).

entries.title: ǂnabbe ta !nu (Corona Australis)
entries.snippet: A girl, when men should not have been looked upon by her for fear of harm to them, saw some people eating together at a rock rabbit's house of branches.

entries.title: ǂnerru and her husband
entries.snippet: A young man of the Early Race marries ǂnerru (also a person of the Early Race). They are out collecting 'Bushman rice' and fill their bag, so the husband asks |nerru to use her kaross.

entries.title: A man falls upon the Lion
entries.snippet: A man is out hunting and is chased by a Lion up a tree where he falls asleep and dreams of falling into the Lion's mouth.

entries.title: Children throwing up the Sun (in this dialect)
entries.snippet: The Sun, a man from whose armpit brightness proceeded, lived formerly on earth; but only gave light for a space around his house.

entries.title: Day's Heart
entries.snippet: The "Dawn's Heart" (the star Jupiter) has a daughter, who is identified with some neighbouring star preceding Jupiter (at the time when we asked, it was Regulus or Alpha Leonis).

entries.title: Dia!kwain's relations
entries.snippet: Notes about Dia!kwain (David Hoesar) and his relations, including information about their places and names as well as details of their personal history.

entries.title: Eclipse of the sun
entries.snippet: An account of an eclipse of the sun. It crosses over into the sky and makes the people's head-hair resemble grass. They stare at the sky and wonder at what the sun is doing to make things appear this way.

entries.title: Heron's song (The song of the Blue Crane)
entries.snippet: The Blue Crane goes along singing that the Krieboom berries are on her shoulder. There are verses for walking slowly, for running away from a man and for flapping her wings.

entries.title: How an Elephant steals a young Springbok from ǀkaggen (the Mantis) or Pet Springbok carried off by an Elephant
entries.snippet: The pet springbok of the Mantis carried off by an Elephant, while the Mantis is in a hole, digging out sweet food for it,-the Elephant substitutes her own calf,-the inarticulate reply of the latter to the questions of…

entries.title: Hyena and lion
entries.snippet: A lion sees the hyena carry away a gemsbok to give to her children waiting in the hole. It goes to head her off but the hyena bites the lion's thigh and it screams in pain.

Jackal's song (and The song of the Caama Fox)

entries.title: Jacob Nein and the leopard
entries.snippet: An account of how a man called Jacob Nein (or |kann) is bitten by a leopard that he was hunting because he and his family were hungry.

entries.title: Leopards and jackals
entries.snippet: The Jackal howls and begs the Leopard for springbok flesh for it to eat. The Jackal angers the Leopard which then kills it and places its body in a bush.

entries.title: Leopards, lions and phrases
entries.snippet: A leopard jumps out of a bush and grasps a man's head in its claws. The man slowly returns to his house to lie down and nurse his wounded head.

entries.title: Lion and Fieldmouse
entries.snippet: The Lion wants the Fieldmouse's chest, which is broader than his own, so that he may make a loader noise when roaring and people may fear him.

entries.title: Lion and Tortoise
entries.snippet: [The two Lions !haue ta ≠hou and !gu] then hunted for food, but in vain. They perceived, at last, a male tortoise, and, notwithstanding its advice, as well as the request of his companion to be allowed to share this…

entries.title: Moon and little Hare (includes: The Moon's speech)
entries.snippet: In [this] version [of the origin of death] (given by |a!kúnta, and apparently unfinished) the Moon appears, at one time, to tell the little Hare that his mother will come to life again, and that, therefore, he need not…

entries.title: Names of stars
entries.snippet: The names of a number of Stars have been ascertained with the kind help of Mr. Maclear. Besides a separate alphabetical index to the Stars, the names of some are given in a supplementary list (B XXV.

entries.title: Old woman's song (2nd version)
entries.snippet: The old She-Hyena carries away the old woman who has been left lying alone in her hut.

entries.title: Personal history of prisoners

entries.title: Rainmaking

entries.title: Resurrection of the He-Ostrich
entries.snippet: A man kills an Ostrich and takes the body home to his wife, who removes its feathers. A gust of wind carries a feather up into the sky after which it falls into the water and gets wet.

entries.title: Stars

entries.title: The Blue Crane and the girls (including the Blue Crane's speech)
entries.snippet: The Blue Crane says to the young girls that she would like to go with them to sit under the great thorn tree where they might kill the lice that are biting her head.

entries.title: The eating of jackal
entries.snippet: The eating of jackal by men and women.

entries.title: The jackal watches the lion
entries.snippet: Jackals watch the lions while they eat the springbok.

entries.title: The lion and the giraffe
entries.snippet: A lion springs up and bites a giraffe who falls on the lion and kills it.

entries.title: The Lion becomes a star
entries.snippet: The (two) Lions, which was the name given to us (by our first informants) for the Pointers to the Southern Cross, were formerly men, and at the same time lions. (LII. -1.

entries.title: The Lions, the Tortoise, the little Hare and the old woman
entries.snippet: [The two Lions !haue ta ≠hou and !gu] came to the house of an old woman who was lame, and lived with a little hare. These also managed to outwit the lions; and, at last, !gu died of starvation.

entries.title: The man carries away the ostrich to his home
entries.snippet: A man carries an ostrich to his house where he and his wife roast its flesh in a cooking pot.

entries.title: The Mantis and !goe !kweitentu
entries.snippet: Among the fights which the Mantis has with different personages and animals, one of the most interesting to the Bushman mind, is that with a being whose eyes are in his feet, instead of his head, which is smooth.

entries.title: The Mantis turned into a hartebeest
entries.snippet: |kaggen (the Mantis) deceives the young girl-children by becoming a hartebeest and feigning death. The children are delighted to find meat and make knives to cut it up.

entries.title: The monster ǁkhwai-hemm's speech
entries.snippet: The visit of the Mantis to the houses of the Ticks, - who, excepting one boy, have hidden themselves away in the fleeces of their sheet, -evil thoughts of the Mantis with regard to this boy, who is left in charge of the…

entries.title: The Moon pierced by the Sun
entries.snippet: Whilst in the preceding myths of the Mantis, the Moon, according to its origin, is only a piece of leather (a shoe of the Mantis),-in Bushman astrological mythology the Moon is looked upon as a man who incurs the wrath…

entries.title: The piercing of ears
entries.snippet: An account of the piercing of ears.

entries.title: The sending of the Crows, or, Crows sent out to look for husbands
entries.snippet: Bushmen women send out crows to ascertain what has become of their husbands who have not returned from hunting; they hang fat round the necks of the crows as food for their journey.

entries.title: The spider
entries.snippet: The healing of spider bites with honey.

entries.title: The ǀxam and the Dutch
entries.snippet: (Dutch) Boers take |xam children to work for them, while the |xam take and eat Boer (Dutch) cattle.

entries.title: The ǀxam and the hyena
entries.snippet: Youths hunt and kill hyena and then the people at home make soup out of their flesh. The people sleep after eating.

entries.title: ǀkaggen (the Mantis) and the Moon (version 1)
entries.snippet: The origin of the Moon is an episode in the story of the Mantis and his pet eland.

entries.title: ǀkaggen (the Mantis) and the Moon: creation of the latter (version 2)
entries.snippet: The Moon is created from a long white wing-feather of the Ostrich. After |kaggen (the Mantis) breaks the eland's gall and it blinds him, he crawls, feeling his way to the place of the Ostrich, where he finds a feather.

entries.title: ǀxam-speaking people

entries.title: ǀxui tatin and the dog

entries.title: ǀxui tatin's story
entries.snippet: Namaquas capture |xui tatin's master while he is working (much is not translated).

entries.title: ǁkabbo (Jantje) in the train
entries.snippet: ||kabbo (Jantje) goes on a journey in a train to Simonstown. He meets a woman who stops him from falling out of the train, as well as a black man and a white man, each of whom he describes.

entries.title: ǁkabbo's (Jantje's) capture
entries.snippet: llkábbo,'s Capture and Journey to Cape Town are twice described by him. One of these accounts (L II.-1. 266-272), the beginning of which is also given in another version. (B II.
